excerpt: The blessed resurrection which is to come: consider, beloved, how the Lord continually proves to us that there will be a future resurrection. The day and the night show us a resurrection; the seed sown in the earth shews a resurrection. Consider the bird called the phoenix — it is created from its own ashes, bearing witness to our resurrection.
notes: Resurrection of the body argued from natural cycles and the phoenix; bodily resurrection teaching

excerpt: To the high priest are given proper services, to the priests their proper place assigned, and on Levites their proper ministrations imposed; the laymen are bound by lay ordinances. Each of us must please God in his own place and not transgress the fixed rule of his service.
notes: Liturgical hierarchy and fixed priestly order as template for Christian ministry

excerpt: The apostles received the gospel from the Lord Jesus Christ; Jesus the Christ was sent from God. So Christ is from God, the apostles from Christ. The apostles went out in the assurance of the Holy Spirit and appointed their first converts as bishops and deacons.
notes: Apostolic appointment of bishops and deacons; earliest succession language

excerpt: Our apostles knew through our Lord Jesus Christ that there would be strife over the title of bishop. For this reason, since they had received complete foreknowledge, they appointed those mentioned above and then gave the instruction that when these died, other approved men should succeed to their ministry.
notes: Apostolic foresight and orderly succession of bishops as established norm

excerpt: Paraphrase: Clement presents Peter and Paul as recent exemplary sufferers, saying Peter endured many labors and Paul bore witness and departed from the world after noble endurance.
The church of God which sojourns at Rome, to the church of God sojourning at Corinth.
notes: Explicit Rome-to-Corinth address establishing both communities

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paul addresses the church of God that is in Corinth.
Paraphrase: Paul recounts the tradition of the Lord taking bread and the cup on the night he was handed over.
Paraphrase: Paul rehearses the received proclamation that Christ died, was buried, was raised, and appeared.
Paraphrase: The churches of Asia greet Corinth, and Aquila and Prisca together with the church in their house send warm greetings in the Lord.
Paraphrase: Paul states that he will remain in Ephesus until Pentecost.

What, after all, is Apollos? And what is Paul? Only servants, through whom you came to believe — as the Lord has assigned to each his task. I planted the seed, Apollos watered it, but God has been making it grow.
Paraphrase: The Lord comes with holy myriads to execute judgment on all.
Paraphrase: Everyone who believes that Jesus is the Christ has been born of God.
Paraphrase: Peter addresses the elect exiles of the dispersion in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia.
Paraphrase: Peter blesses God for giving new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.
She who is in Babylon, chosen together with you, sends you her greetings, and so does my son Mark.
Paraphrase: Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy address the church of the Thessalonians in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
Paraphrase: Paul states that Jesus died and rose again and that God will bring with Jesus those who have died.
To Timothy my true son in the faith: Grace, mercy and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.
Paraphrase: Paul urges Timothy to remain on at Ephesus to charge certain persons not to teach differently.
Paraphrase: Paul addresses the church of God in Corinth together with all the saints in the whole of Achaia.
Paraphrase: Paul says that the one who raised the Lord Jesus will raise believers also with Jesus.
Paraphrase: Titus is Paul partner and fellow worker.
Paraphrase: The true confession is that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh, and those who deny it are deceivers.
Paraphrase: The present heavens and earth are reserved for fire until the day of judgment and destruction of the ungodly.
Paraphrase: Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy address the church of the Thessalonians in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
Paraphrase: Paul urges Timothy to remember Jesus Christ, risen from the dead, as proclaimed in the gospel.
Paraphrase: Paul says Demas has gone to Thessalonica, Crescens to Galatia, Titus to Dalmatia, and Tychicus has been sent to Ephesus.
Paraphrase: Only Luke is with Paul, and Mark is useful for ministry.
Paraphrase: Paul asks Timothy to bring the cloak left with Carpus at Troas when he comes.
Paraphrase: Paul notes that Trophimus was left ill at Miletus.
Paraphrase: The apostles, the women, and Mary the mother of Jesus are gathered in Jerusalem.
Paraphrase: Jesus is taken up as the disciples watch, and messengers tell them he will come in the same way.
Paraphrase: Cornelius of Caesarea sends for Peter, hears the message, and the Holy Spirit falls on Gentiles who are then baptized.
Paraphrase: Barnabas brings Saul to Antioch, where they teach with the church and the disciples are first called Christians.
Paraphrase: Herod Agrippa lays violent hands on members of the church and kills James the brother of John with the sword.
Paraphrase: Herod lays violent hands on members of the church, kills James the brother of John, and arrests Peter in Jerusalem.
Paraphrase: Barnabas and Saul return from Jerusalem, taking John Mark with them.
Paraphrase: In the church at Antioch there were prophets and teachers including Barnabas, Simeon called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen, and Saul.
Paraphrase: Barnabas and Saul sail to Cyprus, proclaim in Salamis, traverse the island to Paphos, and Sergius Paulus believes after Elymas is opposed.
Paraphrase: The Jerusalem council addresses whether Gentile believers must be circumcised and sends its decision in a letter.
Paraphrase: Paul comes to Lystra, where he finds Timothy, a disciple well spoken of by the believers there and in Iconium.
Paraphrase: The mission arrives at Philippi, a Roman colony, and Lydia responds in faith and hospitality.
Paraphrase: Paul and Silas proclaim in Thessalonica and some are persuaded, forming a new believing community.
Paraphrase: Paul and his companions minister in Berea where many receive the message eagerly.
Paraphrase: Paul reasons in Athens and speaks at the Areopagus, with some joining and believing.
Paraphrase: Some mocked Paul's resurrection message in Athens, but others joined him and believed, including Dionysius and Damaris.
Paraphrase: Dionysius the Areopagite, a woman named Damaris, and others join Paul and believe in Athens.
Paraphrase: Opponents in Thessalonica attack Jason's house and drag Jason before the city authorities.
Paraphrase: Paul ministers in Corinth with companions and a new community takes shape there.
Paraphrase: While Gallio is proconsul of Achaia, Paul is brought before the tribunal in Corinth and Sosthenes is beaten before it.
Paraphrase: Paul stays and works with Aquila and Priscilla because they share the same trade.
Paraphrase: Apollos arrives at Ephesus, is instructed more accurately by Priscilla and Aquila, and later assists believers in Achaia.
Paraphrase: Priscilla and Aquila explain the way of God more accurately.
Paraphrase: Paul stays in Corinth with Titius Justus, whose house is next door to the synagogue.
Paraphrase: Paul moves next door to the house of Titius Justus in Corinth, and Crispus the synagogue ruler believes in the Lord with his household.
Paraphrase: Crispus, the ruler of the synagogue in Corinth, believes in the Lord together with his household.
Paraphrase: Paul teaches in Ephesus and the message spreads broadly in the region.
Paraphrase: Paul meets disciples in Ephesus who knew only John's baptism before they are instructed about Jesus and baptized in his name.
Paraphrase: Demetrius the silversmith in Ephesus warns fellow craftsmen that Paul's message threatens their trade and Artemis devotion.
Paraphrase: The crowd in Ephesus seizes Aristarchus, Paul's Macedonian travel companion, in the uproar.
Paraphrase: In Ephesus, Alexander is pushed forward to address the crowd, but the assembly shouts in defense of Artemis.
Paraphrase: Paul withdraws to reason daily in the hall of Tyrannus in Ephesus.
Paraphrase: On the day of Pentecost the gathered disciples are filled with the Holy Spirit and begin speaking in other tongues.
Paraphrase: Peter stands with the Eleven at Pentecost and publicly proclaims the risen Jesus.
Paraphrase: Peter proclaims that God raised Jesus up, and the apostles are witnesses.
Paraphrase: Paul goes by way of Assos, Mitylene, Chios, and Samos on the route toward Miletus.
Paraphrase: Sopater of Berea and Aristarchus and Secundus of Thessalonica are named among Paul's companions.
Paraphrase: Paul stays in Troas, departs by sea, and comes to Miletus where he summons the Ephesian elders.
Paraphrase: A prophet named Agabus comes down from Judea and warns that Paul will be bound in Jerusalem.
Paraphrase: Disciples from Caesarea bring Paul to lodge with Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple.
Paraphrase: Jews in Jerusalem have previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian with Paul in the city.
Paraphrase: Paul lands at Tyre, finds disciples there, and continues onward on the journey to Jerusalem.
Paraphrase: Paul and his companions stay with Philip the evangelist in Caesarea, where his four daughters prophesy.
Paraphrase: Claudius Lysias writes to Felix, and Paul is brought under guard to Caesarea for a hearing before the governor.
Paraphrase: Tertullus presents the case against Paul before Felix in Caesarea on behalf of the Jerusalem leadership.
Paraphrase: Felix comes with Drusilla in Caesarea, hears Paul about faith in Christ, and keeps him in custody hoping for a bribe.
Paraphrase: Festus arrives in the province, goes from Caesarea to Jerusalem, and returns to Caesarea to hear Paul's case.
Paraphrase: Agrippa and Bernice come to Caesarea, where Festus presents Paul's case and a formal hearing is arranged.
Paraphrase: Paul is handed over to a centurion named Julius, and the party puts in at Sidon where Julius treats Paul kindly.
Paraphrase: Running under the lee of the small island called Clauda, the crew secures the ship's boat and undergirds the vessel.
Paraphrase: Paul voyage under guard touches Sidon, passes Cnidus, and reaches Fair Havens while debate continues about wintering at Phoenix.
Paraphrase: Paul comes to Myra in Lycia, then sails under Crete past Salmone and reaches Fair Havens.
Paraphrase: Paul is received on Malta and then sails onward by way of Syracuse, Rhegium, and Puteoli toward Rome.
Paraphrase: Believers come from Rome to meet Paul as far as Appii Forum and Three Taverns.
Paraphrase: Paul remains in Rome under guard while proclaiming the kingdom and teaching openly.
Paraphrase: Publius, the leading man of the island, receives Paul and his companions on Malta, and healings follow.
Paraphrase: Stephen is stoned outside Jerusalem after his witness before the council.
Paraphrase: A great persecution breaks out against the church in Jerusalem, and Saul ravages the church house by house.
Paraphrase: Philip proclaims Christ in Samaria, many believe, and Peter and John pray for the new Samaritan believers.

Now for some time a man named Simon had practised sorcery in the city and amazed all the people of Samaria. He boasted that he was someone great... Philip proclaimed the Christ; Simon himself believed and was baptized. When Simon saw that the Spirit was given at the laying on of the apostles' hands, he offered them money, saying 'Give me also this ability.' But Peter answered: 'May your money perish with you, because you thought you could buy the gift of God with money.'
Paraphrase: Saul encounter on the way to Damascus leads to his reversal, healing, and baptism.
Paraphrase: In Damascus, the disciple Ananias restores Saul's sight, addresses his calling, and baptizes him.
Paraphrase: In Lydda, Peter finds Aeneas bedridden for years and declares Jesus Christ has healed him.
Paraphrase: Peter ministers in the coastal plain and at Joppa, where many turn to the Lord after Tabitha is raised.
Paraphrase: In Joppa, the disciple Tabitha dies and is raised, and many in the city believe in the Lord.
Paraphrase: Peter stays many days in Joppa with Simon the tanner.

Paraphrase: The Hymn of the Pearl tells of a prince sent to Egypt to retrieve a pearl guarded by a serpent; he forgets his mission until a letter reminds him; he seizes the pearl and returns to his father's kingdom, receiving his glorious robe; widely read as an allegory of the soul's descent and return.
notes: Hymn of the Pearl

excerpt: Paraphrase: Thomas anoints the convert with oil, invoking the name of Christ; the anointing precedes the water baptism; oil is called the seal of the covenant and the power of the Messiah.
notes: Baptismal anointing scene

excerpt: Paraphrase: Thomas breaks bread and gives thanks over the eucharist; the elements are bread and water (not wine in the Syriac recension); continence and renunciation of the world are required of those who receive.
notes: Eucharist and encratite practice

excerpt: Paraphrase: Faith is the foundation upon which all the structure of Christian life is built; faith is the tree from which the fruits of good works grow; faith rests on the rock of Christ and is watered by baptism; the faithful must believe in God, his Christ, the Holy Spirit, baptism, resurrection, and eternal life.
notes: On Faith — foundation of Christian life

excerpt: Paraphrase: Jesus is the promised Messiah of the prophets; he is called Son of God, Firstborn, the Word, and the Image of God; Aphrahat demonstrates from Scripture that the Messiah was to suffer, die, and rise again; the Jews who reject him have stumbled over the stone.
notes: On Jesus the Messiah

excerpt: Paraphrase: Virginity is a higher calling; those who consecrate themselves in virginity become temples of the Holy Spirit; even marriage is honorable, but the virgin is closer to the angels; Aphrahat draws on Paul's counsel in 1 Corinthians 7.
notes: On Virginity

excerpt: Paraphrase: Aphrahat addresses the Christians suffering under Shapur II's persecution; he encourages steadfastness, reminding them that persecution purifies the church; the martyrs receive a crown; God will vindicate his people as he did Israel in Egypt.
notes: On Persecution under Shapur II

excerpt: Paraphrase: Aphrahat teaches that true fasting is not merely abstaining from food but from sin and wickedness; the fast that God desires involves feeding the hungry, clothing the naked, and sheltering the stranger; bodily fasting disciplines the flesh for prayer.
notes: On Fasting

excerpt: Paraphrase: Prayer should be unceasing and offered at all times; the one who prays must be pure in heart and free from anger; prayer is the soul's communion with God and the means by which the faithful receive the Holy Spirit.
notes: On Prayer

excerpt: Paraphrase: Aphrahat describes the bnay qyama (members of the covenant) — baptized Christians who take on a life of ascetic consecration including celibacy and renunciation; they are the holy ones who form the heart of the church; marriage is permitted but the covenant life is the higher calling.
notes: On the ascetic covenant (bnay qyama)

excerpt: Paraphrase: Those who have sinned after baptism may return to God through genuine repentance; the penitent must confess with a broken heart; God receives the penitent just as the father received the prodigal son.
notes: On Penitents

locator: Demonstration 8 (On the Resurrection of the Dead)
excerpt: Paraphrase: Aphrahat defends bodily resurrection against those who deny it; as the seed that falls into the ground rises again, so shall the dead rise; Christ's resurrection is the guarantee and firstfruits of the general resurrection.
notes: On Resurrection

excerpt: Paraphrase: God is not born, not made, an ever-abiding nature without beginning or end, immortal, perfect, and incomprehensible; Christians trace their religion from Jesus the Messiah Son of God Most High who came down from heaven and from a Hebrew virgin assumed and clothed himself with flesh; the Son of God lived in a daughter of man.

Paraphrase: Christians keep commandments written for them; they do not commit adultery or fornication; they do not bear false witness; they love one another; they give to the poor; they visit strangers and the sick; they fast two or three days; they pray; they are pure in speech.

Paraphrase: If any Christian is poor and needy, Christians fast two or three days so the needy may be supplied with necessities; they observe the commandments of their Lord with care; they live righteously and justly; truly these are the people who have found the truth.
Paraphrase: Athanasius recounts how Frumentius was consecrated bishop for the Indians (Aksumites) and how Constantius demanded Frumentius be sent for re-examination by the Arian bishop George.
notes: Athanasius's own account of Frumentius's consecration and Constantius's letter.

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Son of God is the Logos and mind of the Father; the Son is in the Father and the Father in the Son in unity and power of Spirit; the Holy Spirit is an effluence from God as light flows from fire; all three are distinguished yet united.

Paraphrase: Christians each have one wife or none; second marriage is adultery; those who marry do so only for the sake of children; they esteem chastity and virginity highly; their aim is to dwell with God, not to gratify passion.

Paraphrase: Christians pray for the emperor and his government; they obey those who rule in all things except what would harm piety; they offer rational worship and prayers, not animal sacrifices; they ask only to be judged by their deeds.

Paraphrase: God is uncreated and eternal, to be beheld by the understanding alone; we acknowledge one God who has made all things by the Logos which is from Him; He is the God who framed all things by the Logos and holds them in being by His Spirit — not atheists but worshippers of the one Creator.

Paraphrase: Arguments defending the truth of resurrection must precede all inquiry about it; God who created what did not exist can certainly restore what has been dissolved; disbelief in resurrection has no reasonable foundation.

Paraphrase: The body must rise together with the soul to receive just judgment; the same composite human nature that lived and acted must stand before God at the final judgment; soul alone is incomplete for this purpose.

Regarding water, the Scripture speaks concerning Israel: woe to those who have abandoned the living spring and dug themselves cisterns. Baptism is a holy spring of life. We descend into the water laden with sin but come up bearing fruit.
notes: OT baptism typology; water as life-giving spring; regeneration language

excerpt: There are two ways of teaching and of power, the one of light and the other of darkness; and there is a great difference between the two ways. For on the one are stationed the light-giving angels of God, on the other the angels of Satan.
notes: Opening of the Two Ways catechesis (chs. 18-20); functions as pre-baptismal moral instruction

excerpt: The way of light: love your Creator, glorify him who redeemed you from death. You shall fast and pray for the salvation of your soul. You shall not forsake the commandments of the Lord. You shall confess your transgressions.
notes: Two-ways instruction with fasting as explicit requirement

excerpt: Since therefore He was about to be made manifest in the flesh and to suffer, His suffering was foreordained. The prophet speaks: Spare my soul from the sword, and fasten my flesh with nails; for the congregation of evildoers hath risen up against me.
notes: Christ's suffering in the flesh as prophetically foreordained; incarnation theology

excerpt: Being baptized, we are illuminated; illuminated, we become sons; being made sons, we are perfected; being perfected, we are made immortal. This work is variously called grace, and illumination, and perfection, and washing: washing, by which we cleanse away our sins; grace, by which the penalties accruing to transgressions are remitted; and illumination, by which that holy light of salvation is beheld, that is, by which we see God clearly.
notes: Baptism as illumination and new birth; the fourfold name of baptism; adoption as children of God

excerpt: Paraphrase: Clement defends marriage as God-ordained and honorable for the begetting of children, citing Paul that it is better to marry than to burn; yet he honors continence as a higher calling when freely chosen for the love of God, insisting both states are legitimate and neither is to be despised.
notes: Marriage defended; celibacy honored as higher calling; against both encratite rejection and licentiousness

excerpt: The blood of the Lord is twofold. For there is the blood of His flesh, by which we are redeemed from corruption; and the spiritual, that by which we are anointed. And to drink the blood of Jesus is to become partaker of the Lord's immortality; the Spirit being the energetic principle of the Word, as blood is of flesh. The mixture of water and Word is called Eucharist, renowned and glorious grace; and they who by faith partake of it are sanctified both in body and soul.
notes: Eucharistic blood of Christ; real and spiritual redemption through the Eucharist; body and soul sanctified

excerpt: Let spectacles, then, and plays that are full of scurrility and of abundant mimicry, be fled from. For what base action is there that is not exhibited in the theatres? And what shameless saying is there which is not brought forward by the buffoons? And those who enjoy the evil stamp the image of it at home. And the ears are to be kept from pollution; since I think it wrong even to look on those things, much more to hear them.
notes: Christians must avoid theaters, games, and public spectacles; moral contamination through entertainment

excerpt: Paraphrase: Clement instructs that frugality of diet and sobriety of living constitute the ascetic discipline of the Christian; the body is to be trained by simple food, temperance, and voluntary fasting, so that the soul may be free to pursue wisdom and prayer without the hindrance of luxury or fleshly desire.
notes: Ascetic discipline; fasting and temperance for the sake of the soul; against bodily luxury

excerpt: The New Song has sprung up, eternal, which has also become new for the new people — the Lord himself, who, being a new light above the sun, has risen upon those living in darkness and the shadow of death. Behold the might of the new song! It has made men out of stones, men out of beasts. Those who were as dead, not being partakers of the true life, have come to life again, simply by becoming listeners to this song.
notes: Christ as the divine new song; pre-existent Logos incarnate calling all to life and salvation

excerpt: Paraphrase: Clement's closing exhortation calls hearers to abandon darkness and receive the divine Logos through faith and baptism; the new song — Christ himself — illuminates, adopts as sons, and leads all who receive him into the eternal light of the Father; conversion culminates in baptismal illumination.
notes: Final call to conversion, baptism, and illumination; Protrepticus as pre-baptismal address to pagans

excerpt: The renunciation of wealth is not about physically disposing of money but about stripping the soul of its passions. Wealth is a tool; rightly used it becomes an instrument for doing good and gaining friends who will intercede for you.

God has given earthly wealth to the rich so that it may be distributed to those in need. Almsgiving redeems sins and enlists the prayers of the poor on one's behalf. Love of neighbor, expressed through giving, is the second great commandment.

John the Apostle, returning to Ephesus after his exile, sought out a young man who had lapsed into banditry. He pursued him, restored him to the church, and accepted him back through repentance — showing that post-baptismal sin is not beyond forgiveness.

Before the advent of the Lord, philosophy was necessary to the Greeks for righteousness. And now it becomes conducive to piety; being a kind of preparatory training to those who attain to faith through demonstration. For God is the cause of all good things; but of some primarily, as of the Old and the New Testament; and of others by consequence, as philosophy. Perchance, too, philosophy was given to the Greeks directly and primarily, till the Lord should call the Greeks. For this was a schoolmaster to bring the Hellenic mind, as the law the Hebrews, to Christ.
notes: Greek philosophy as divinely permitted preparatory schoolmaster for Gentiles; parallel to Mosaic Law

excerpt: Paraphrase: Clement rejects the encratite condemnation of marriage while explicitly praising virginity undertaken for God as the nobler vocation; both marriage practised with self-restraint and celibacy freely chosen are honored as legitimate Christian callings; neither creation nor the body is to be despised.
notes: Both marriage and celibacy are legitimate; virginity explicitly honored as higher calling; against encratite extremes

excerpt: They preserve the tradition of the blessed teaching derived directly from the holy apostles, Peter, James, John, and Paul, the son receiving it from the father; came by God's will to us also to deposit those ancestral and apostolic seeds. And they are rightly called gnostics who have been nurtured in this ecclesiastical and apostolic tradition. The true gnostic, in my opinion, is he who has grown old in the Scriptures, and maintains the apostolic and ecclesiastical standard of doctrine.
notes: Apostolic tradition preserved through teacher succession from Peter, James, John, and Paul; rule of faith

excerpt: The spiritual person prays in thought during every hour, being united with God through love. And throughout his whole life he sings hymns to the Almighty, speaking in the Holy Spirit; not in a fixed place, or selected temple, or at certain festivals and appointed days, but during his whole life, in every place — whether walking, conversing, in silence, in reading or in works, he offers prayer in every way.
notes: Continual unceasing prayer as the mark of the spiritually formed Christian; prayer in all places at all times

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paul addresses the saints and faithful brothers and sisters in Christ in Colossae.
Paraphrase: The Colossians learned the gospel from Epaphras, a faithful minister.
Paraphrase: Believers were buried with Christ in baptism and raised with him through faith in God's power.
Paraphrase: Epaphras is one of them and labors for Colossae, Laodicea, and Hierapolis.
Paraphrase: Paul sends greetings to the believers in Laodicea, to Nympha, and to the church in her house.
Paraphrase: Paul instructs that the letter be read in Laodicea and that the Colossians also read the letter from Laodicea.
Paraphrase: Paul tells the Colossians to say to Archippus that he should fulfill the ministry he received in the Lord.
Paraphrase: Tychicus is the beloved brother and faithful minister sent to the Colossians with Onesimus, who is one of them.
Paraphrase: Onesimus, one of the Colossians, is sent with Tychicus and identified as a faithful and beloved brother.
Paraphrase: Entrants to the community turn from evil and bind themselves to the covenant.

Paraphrase: Among the bishops who signed the acts of the Council of Arles in 314 were three from Britain: Eborius bishop of York, Restitutus bishop of London, and Adelfius bishop of a third see, accompanied by a priest Sacerdos and a deacon Arminius.
notes: Earliest firm documentary attestation of organized British Christianity with 3 episcopal sees.

excerpt: Paraphrase: I myself was held in bonds by the innumerable errors of my previous life and did not believe I could be freed from them. But after the stain of former years had been washed away by the aid of the water of new birth, a light from above poured itself upon my heart now cleansed and purified; after the second birth had restored me to a new man by means of the Spirit breathed from heaven, in a marvellous manner what had been doubtful became certain, what had been hidden was revealed.
notes: Cyprian's conversion and baptismal regeneration testimony; Spirit transformation

excerpt: Paraphrase: Moreover, when we say 'Give us this day our daily bread,' we may understand this both spiritually and literally. For the bread of life is Christ, and this bread belongs not to all, but to us. And as we say 'Our Father' because he is the Father of those who understand and believe, so we call it 'our bread' because Christ is the bread of those who are in his body. And we ask that this bread be given to us daily, lest we who are in Christ and daily receive the Eucharist for the food of salvation should be separated from his body.
notes: Eucharist as daily bread; daily communion practice; Christ's body as ecclesial and sacramental

excerpt: Paraphrase: Let us who are in Christ — that is, who always pray — not fail from prayer at any times or seasons. When we gather for the divine celebrations with the priest of God, let us guard our prayer with modesty and discipline. Let the knees bend in supplication and the voice be restrained. Before us stands the priest, who offers the sacrifice before God. How much more ought we to listen humbly, and give attention to the prayers being offered.
notes: Priestly offering of sacrifice; liturgical discipline; eucharistic sacrifice language

excerpt: Paraphrase: Before all things, the Teacher of peace and Master of unity would not have prayer to be made singly and individually, as if a person prays only for himself alone. We do not say 'My Father who art in heaven' nor 'Give me this day my daily bread.' Our prayer is public and common; and when we pray, we pray not for one, but for the whole people, because we the whole people are one.
notes: Communal nature of Christian prayer; 'Our Father' reflects ecclesial unity

excerpt: Paraphrase: But in respect of the case of infants, which you say ought not to be baptized within the second or third day after their birth, and that the old law of circumcision should be regarded, so that you think one who is just born should not be baptized and sanctified within the eighth day — we all thought very differently in our council. For in this course which you thought was to be taken, no one agreed; but we all rather judged that the mercy and grace of God ought to be denied to no man born.
notes: Council of Carthage 252 ruling: infant baptism not to be delayed; grace denied to none

excerpt: Paraphrase: For if even to the greatest sinners, and to those who had sinned much against God, when they afterwards believed, remission of sins is granted — and nobody is hindered from baptism and from grace — how much rather ought we not to hinder an infant, who being lately born has not sinned, except in that, being born after the flesh according to Adam, he has contracted the contagion of the ancient death at its earliest birth. The infant approaches more easily to the reception of the forgiveness of sins because the sins remitted are not his own but another's.
notes: Infant baptism grounded in original sin/contagion from Adam; grace available to newborns

excerpt: Paraphrase: What then shall we say of the tradition of certain persons who receive the baptism of heretics? Is tradition greater than truth? Stephen claims to follow tradition. But we answer: from the beginning this was the practice, established first by the Lord and then observed by the apostles. The practice arose first, the truth was before custom.
notes: Truth over custom; apostolic precedent for rebaptism; response to Stephen's appeal to tradition

excerpt: Paraphrase: Nor ought custom which had crept in among some to prevent the truth from prevailing and conquering. For custom without truth is the antiquity of error. Let us return to the head and origin of divine tradition. For human error must cease.
notes: Custom without truth is antiquity of error; appeal to divine tradition over mere practice

excerpt: Paraphrase: If baptism could be given by heretics, they could also give the remission of sins. But since it is only in the Church that sins are remitted, baptism cannot be administered outside the Church. For there is one baptism which is appointed in the Catholic Church; and it is not possible that what is done outside should be counted valid for those who are within.
notes: One baptism in the one Church; heretical baptism invalid; no Spirit outside the Church

excerpt: Paraphrase: She who has adorned herself for the purpose of pleasing men and attracting their gaze, even though her body remains undefiled and her virginity preserved, her corrupt mind has perished. The discipline of modesty and temperance belongs not to the body alone but also to the mind.
notes: Modesty as interior discipline; ascetic formation of virgins

excerpt: Paraphrase: The discipline of virginity is the flower of the Church's garden, the glory of spiritual grace, the joy of nature, an illustrious portion of Christ's flock. Virgins are the more illustrious portion of the Church. As they begin here on earth to be what we shall hereafter be, they already possess the glory of the resurrection.
notes: Honor of virginity; virgins as foretaste of resurrection glory

excerpt: Paraphrase: But to those who have stood firm and have not been overthrown, what great joy! To those who have lapsed, medicine is not to be denied; but the lapsed must not assume that peace can be given them hastily by anyone. Let them knock at the door of the Church, but not with violence to break it down; let them beg but not presumptuously demand. Proper penance under the bishop's authority must precede reconciliation.
notes: Graded penance under episcopal authority; against hasty reconciliation by confessors

excerpt: Paraphrase: Some who had received libelli from the confessors or who came to communion without proper reconciliation through the bishop — when they approached the altar to receive, they were stricken: one woman who tried to open the chest containing the Lord's reserved sacrament was deterred by fire rising from it; another who received unworthily vomited up the sanctified cup.
notes: Eucharistic discipline; divine punishment for unworthy communion; reserved sacrament reference

excerpt: Paraphrase: How many bishops who ought to have been an encouragement and example to others, despising their divine stewardship, became agents of secular affairs, forsook their thrones, deserted their people, wandered about over foreign provinces, sought the markets for trade. While their brethren were starving in the Church, they wished to have money in abundance.
notes: Bishops who lapsed through worldly accommodation; failure of episcopal leadership during Decian crisis

excerpt: I know only one baptism in the Church, and I know none outside the Church. This one will be where there is the true hope and the certain faith. For thus it is written: One faith, one hope, one baptism (Eph. 4:5). Not among heretics where there is no hope and a false faith.
notes: Individual sententium affirming rebaptism; Ephesians 4:5 cited as proof text

excerpt: My opinion is fully expressed in the letter which was written to our colleague Jubaianus — that heretics who according to the evangelical and apostolic testimony are called adversaries of Christ and antichrists, when they come to the Church, must be baptized with the one baptism of the Church, that they may be made of adversaries friends, and of antichrists Christians.
notes: Cyprian's own final vote: heretics must be rebaptized; adversaries made friends by the one Church baptism

excerpt: Paraphrase: Cyprian said: You have heard, most beloved colleagues, what Jubaianus our fellow bishop wrote to me, consulting my moderate ability about the unlawful and profane baptism of heretics, and what I wrote back to him. Let each of us now bring forward what we think on this matter, judging no man nor rejecting any from the right of communion if he should think differently from us. For none of us sets himself up as a bishop of bishops or by tyrannical terror forces his colleagues to obedience.
notes: Episcopal collegiality; no bishop of bishops; conciliar freedom of conscience

excerpt: Paraphrase: He who does not hold this unity of the Church — does he think that he holds the faith? He who strives against and resists the Church, does he trust that he is in the Church? He who forsakes the chair of Peter upon whom the Church was built, does he believe himself to be in the Church? For the Lord says: I and the Father are one. And again of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit it is written: And these three are one. Whoever breaks the peace and concord of Christ works against Christ.
notes: Schism and heresy sever from the Church; trinitarian unity mirrors ecclesial unity

excerpt: Paraphrase: They cannot dwell with God who would not be of one mind in God's Church. Although they burn, though cast into fire and flames they give their bodies, that will not be the crown of faith but the punishment of faithlessness. Such a person may be slain; he cannot be crowned. He professes himself to be a Christian in such a way as the devil often feigns himself to be Christ.
notes: Schismatic martyrdom not salvific; unity necessary even unto death

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Lord says to Peter: I say unto thee that thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my Church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it. And to thee will I give the keys of the kingdom of heaven. Upon him he builds the Church, and to him he gives the command to feed the sheep. And although he assigns an equal power to all the apostles, yet he established one chair, and arranged by his authority the source and reason of unity. The other apostles were also what Peter was, but the primacy is given to Peter to show that there is one Church and one chair.
notes: Petrine primacy as source of episcopal unity; one chair, one Church

excerpt: Paraphrase: The episcopate is one, each part of which is held by each one for the whole. The Church also is one, which is spread abroad far and wide into a multitude by an increase of fruitfulness. As the sun has many rays but one light, and a tree has many branches but one strength, and as many streams flow from one spring — so also the Church, bathed in the light of the Lord, extends her rays over the whole world, yet it is one light that is diffused everywhere.
notes: Episcopate held in solidum; Church unity through episcopal collegiality; sun/tree/stream metaphors

excerpt: Paraphrase: The text calls the faithful to return and seek God with a whole heart.

On the Lord's Day come together, break bread, hold Eucharist after confessing transgressions so offering may be pure. Let none with quarrel join until reconciled, that sacrifice not be defiled. In every place and time offer me a pure sacrifice.
notes: Sunday eucharist with sacrifice language and reconciliation requirement

excerpt: Appoint for yourselves bishops and deacons worthy of the Lord, gentle men who are not fond of money, truthful and tested; for they also perform for you the service of the prophets and teachers. Do not despise them, for they are your honorable men together with the prophets and teachers.
notes: Mandate to appoint bishops and deacons; episcopal/diaconal ministry set on par with prophets and teachers

excerpt: Baptism instruction: baptize in the name of Father, Son, Holy Spirit in running water; if not available use other water; if not cold then warm; if neither pour water three times on the head. Candidate and baptizer fast beforehand.
notes: Direct baptismal instruction with flexible water accommodation

excerpt: Do not let your fasts coincide with those of the hypocrites. They fast on Monday and Thursday, but you should fast on Wednesday and Friday.
notes: Mandated Christian fasting schedule distinguishing community from Jewish practice

excerpt: Eucharist thanksgiving over cup (holy vine of David) and broken bread (life and knowledge through Jesus). Let none eat or drink except those baptized in the Lord's name.
notes: Early eucharistic prayer with baptism prerequisite

excerpt: Paraphrase: Concerning fasting, Christians should fast during the days of the Passion; the proper place in the church-building is prescribed; the Sabbath rest is given symbolic content and Christians are admonished to treat every day as belonging to the Lord.
notes: Fasting and liturgical rules

excerpt: Paraphrase: Rules about deacons and deaconesses and widows; deaconesses are appointed for the ministry to women, especially for baptismal anointing; widows are maintained by the church and must devote themselves to prayer.
notes: Deacons, deaconesses, and widows

excerpt: Paraphrase: The bishop must be blameless, a shepherd who feeds the flock with the word of God; he judges sinners and receives penitents; he is the mediator between God and the people; the most salient feature is the exaltation of episcopal authority.
notes: Bishops' authority and qualifications

excerpt: Paraphrase: Even great sinners, upon repentance, are to be received with kindness; no sins are excepted; the canonical penance is to be of two to seven weeks; the bishop must imitate God who forgives.
notes: Penance and reconciliation

excerpt: Paraphrase: Before baptism, the catechumen is anointed with oil by the laying on of hands by the bishop; after anointing the convert is baptized with the proper trinitarian invocation; deaconesses anoint women candidates; the convert is then permitted to partake of the eucharist.
notes: Baptismal anointing and deaconesses

excerpt: Christians are not distinguished from other men by country or language or customs. But while dwelling in Greek and barbarian cities, and following the local customs, they display to us their wonderful and confessedly striking method of life. They dwell in their own countries, but simply as sojourners. As citizens they share in all things, and yet endure all things as if foreigners. Every foreign land is to them as their native country, and every land of their birth as a land of strangers.
notes: Christians as a distinct third people; resident aliens in every nation; sociological description of early Christianity

excerpt: He sent the very Creator and Fashioner of all things, by whom he made the heavens; by whom he enclosed the sea within its proper bounds; whose mysteries all the elements faithfully observe; from whom the sun has received the measure of his daily course; this is He whom He sent to them.
notes: God sent the Logos, Creator of all things, as his envoy; high Logos Christology

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paul addresses the saints in Ephesus, though the phrase 'in Ephesus' is textually debated.

Paraphrase: Ephrem opens his commentary on the harmonized gospel by confessing Christ as the Word made flesh who united divinity and humanity; the four gospels woven into one witness reveal the single plan of salvation; Ephrem reads each episode christologically and typologically.
notes: Opening — christological interpretation

excerpt: Paraphrase: Ephrem comments on the Last Supper in the Diatessaron: Christ took bread and called it his body; he took the cup and called it his blood of the new covenant; the eucharist is the medicine of life given to heal mortal humanity.
notes: Last Supper — eucharistic theology

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Son clothed himself in a body from Mary; he who is invisible became visible; he who fills all things was contained in a womb; Ephrem celebrates the incarnation as the divine condescension that makes salvation possible.
notes: Incarnation — divine condescension

excerpt: Paraphrase: In baptism the believer puts on Christ as a garment of glory; the oil of anointing seals the gift of the Spirit; the eucharistic bread is the medicine of life and the cup is the blood that purifies; through these mysteries the church participates in Christ's death and resurrection.
notes: Baptism and eucharist as mysteries

excerpt: Paraphrase: Ephrem explores the mystery of the Trinity through paradox: the Father begets the Son without division; the Spirit proceeds without separation; the three are one nature yet three persons; human language fails before the divine mystery but faith embraces what reason cannot grasp.
notes: Trinity — paradox and mystery

excerpt: Paraphrase: Ephrem denounces Bardaisan for mixing truth with error: Bardaisan confessed one God but taught that matter and darkness are co-eternal; his hymns seduced the people of Edessa; Ephrem composed counter-hymns in the same meters to win back the faithful.
notes: Against Bardaisan — free will and cosmology

excerpt: Paraphrase: Ephrem refutes Marcion and Mani together: the Creator God is good, not an inferior demiurge; the body is not evil but created by God; Christ truly took flesh; the Old Testament and New Testament proclaim the same God.
notes: Against Marcion and Mani — creation and incarnation

excerpt: Paraphrase: Blessed is the night in which the Pure One came forth to sinners; the Lord of all entered through a lowly maid; the Infinite made himself small in the womb of Mary; Ephrem weaves paradox upon paradox celebrating the mystery of the incarnation.
notes: Virgin birth and incarnation paradox

excerpt: Paraphrase: Mary is the new Eve: as Eve brought death through the tree, Mary brought life through her Son; the garden of Eden finds its counterpart in Bethlehem; the curse of Eve is overturned by the blessing of Mary.
notes: Mary-Eve typology

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paradise is a great mountain rising above all the earth; at its summit dwells the Tree of Life; the church on earth is a reflection of Paradise; through baptism the faithful re-enter the garden that Adam lost.
notes: Eden and Church typology

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Tree of Life stands veiled in glory; its fruit gives immortality; in the eucharist the faithful taste the fruit of that tree; the garments of glory lost by Adam are restored in baptism.
notes: Tree of Life and eschatological restoration

excerpt: Paraphrase: Eusebius records that Paul was beheaded in Rome itself and Peter likewise suffered under Nero, appealing to Roman memorial traditions and earlier witnesses for their martyrdoms.

Paraphrase: Eusebius reproduces documents from the archives of Edessa: King Abgar writes to Jesus asking for healing; Jesus replies promising to send a disciple after his ascension; Thomas later sends Thaddaeus (Addai) who heals Abgar and evangelizes Edessa.
notes: Abgar-Jesus correspondence and Addai tradition

excerpt: Gaius, in a dialogue against Proclus the Montanist, speaks of Cerinthus, who by revelations pretended were by a great apostle brought forth marvellous tales. Cerinthus taught that after the resurrection there would be an earthly kingdom of Christ. The apostle John, on one occasion going to bathe, and learning that Cerinthus was within, sprang out of the bath and fled, saying he could not endure being under the same roof as one who denied the truth of the Lord's incarnation.

Paraphrase: Eusebius introduces Papias as bishop of Hierapolis and author of five books of Dominical Oracles; he quotes Papias saying he preferred the living oral voice to what was in books, and that he inquired after what Andrew, Peter, Philip, Thomas, James, John, and Matthew said.
notes: Eusebius locates Papias at Hierapolis and confirms his method of collecting oral tradition from those who knew the apostles

excerpt: Under Hyginus, bishop of Rome, Cerdo arrived and taught that the God proclaimed by the law and prophets was not the Father of Christ. After him Marcion of Pontus came to Rome and advanced Cerdo's school, blaspheming the Creator. The Ebionites held Christ to be a mere man born of Joseph.
notes: Eusebius locates Cerdo and Marcion at Rome; names Pontus as Marcion's origin; Ebionites noted

excerpt: Valentinus came to Rome in the time of Hyginus, flourished under Pius, and remained until Anicetus. Cerdo, his predecessor in heresy, had also established himself at Rome under Hyginus. Hegesippus records that Valentinus, Marcion, and others who taught contrary to the truth were active in Rome during this period, each founding a distinct school. Valentinus claimed to have received secret knowledge from Theudas, a disciple of Paul, through which he constructed his elaborate system of divine Aeons.

Hegesippus, in his five Memoirs, states that on a journey to Rome he met many bishops along the way and received the same doctrine from all of them. At Rome he compiled a succession list and remained through the episcopate of Anicetus. He found the same law, the prophets, and the Lord preached everywhere.
notes: Hegesippus travels from Jerusalem to Rome; compiles succession lists verifying apostolic faith; stays through Anicetus

excerpt: There is extant also another epistle of Dionysius, bishop of Corinth, written to the Romans, addressed to Soter who was bishop at that time. We cannot do better than quote the words in which he expresses himself regarding the epistle of Clement to the Corinthians: 'Today we have passed the Lord's holy day, in which we have read your epistle. From it we shall be able to draw admonition at all times... as also the earlier epistle which was written to us through Clement.'

In the same epistle Dionysius speaks of Paul and Peter: 'Both of them planted in our Corinth and taught us jointly. Having taught together in Italy, they suffered martyrdom at the same time.' This same Dionysius also wrote to the Athenians a letter of admonition, almost bringing them back to the faith, which they had abandoned as if after the martyrdom of Publius their bishop.

Paraphrase: Eusebius reports that Bardaisan of Edessa was at first a follower of Valentinus but later turned against the Valentinian heresy; he composed many dialogues in Syriac and Greek against the Marcionites and other heretics; despite his eloquent defense of the faith, he did not wholly purge himself of the old heresy.
notes: Bardaisan of Edessa

excerpt: At the time of the Emperor Hadrian the heresiarchs arose to plague the Church. Saturninus came from Antioch and Basilides from Alexandria. From that time the so-called Gnostic heresy spread like a pestilence. Basilides feigned loftier mysteries and procured the downfall of many in Alexandria; he taught that Christ had not truly suffered but that Simon of Cyrene was substituted and crucified. Saturninus taught that the Saviour was unborn and bodiless, appearing only in the semblance of a man.

A man named Pantaenus was put in charge of the catechetical school at Alexandria, a school whose existence is attested from ancient times. This man had been trained in the Stoic system of philosophy; tradition reports he went as far as India to preach Christ to the Brahmans. Clement of Alexandria names him in his own writings as his instructor, showing that Pantaenus stood in the succession of teachers at Alexandria.

Paraphrase: Pantaenus is said to have gone to India where he found persons who knew of Christ. Bartholomew one of the apostles had preached to them and left with them the writing of Matthew in the Hebrew language which they had preserved till that time.
notes: Eusebius on Pantaenus finding Christians in India with a Hebrew Matthew from Bartholomew.

excerpt: At this time in Phrygia, Montanus, as he was called, together with Alcibiades and Theodotus, gave the adversary occasion to suppose that he was the Paraclete. He was seized with frenzy and began to babble and utter strange sounds, prophesying contrary to the established custom of the Church handed down from the beginning. Of those who listened to this spurious utterance, some were vexed and rebuked him as one that was possessed and controlled by a demon — but others were elated, thinking this was the gift of the Spirit.

Maximilla, likewise, said: 'I am driven from among the sheep like a wolf; I am no wolf — I am word, spirit, and power.' These prophecies of Montanus and Maximilla were opposed by those who took their stand on the tradition of the apostles. The anti-Montanist writer notes that he himself saw Maximilla's prophecies fail: she predicted wars and confusion, but peace followed after her death.

Paraphrase: Irenaeus wrote to Victor of Rome urging peace over the Easter controversy; he noted that diverse custom on this point does not destroy the unity of the faith, citing Polycarp's visit to Anicetus as a precedent for peaceful disagreement; the difference in fasting does not harm the faith.
notes: Irenaeus mediates Quartodeciman controversy; cites Polycarp's visit to Rome as precedent; peace over uniformity

excerpt: Paraphrase: Origen's fame spread throughout the whole world; Beryllus of Bostra, Alexander of Jerusalem, and many other bishops invited him to teach and dispute with heretics; in Caesarea he was ordained presbyter by the bishops of Palestine, which provoked the jealousy of Demetrius of Alexandria.
notes: Eusebius on Origen's fame, ordination controversy, and network of episcopal supporters

excerpt: Paraphrase: Eusebius reports that Hippolytus was a bishop somewhere and that he composed various works. In HE 6.43, Eusebius preserves a letter of Cornelius of Rome describing Novatian's ordination as rival bishop and the condemnation of his rigorist position by a Roman synod of sixty bishops.
notes: Eusebius testimony to Hippolytus's episcopacy and works; Cornelius's letter on Novatian schism; Roman synod against Novatian

excerpt: Paraphrase: Origen, while still very young, succeeded Clement as head of the Catechetical School in Alexandria; so many came to him, both men and women, that a multitude of heretics and not a few of the most distinguished philosophers attended his instruction; he led many to the faith and some to martyrdom.
notes: Eusebius on Origen's early leadership of the Catechetical School

excerpt: Paraphrase: Eusebius preserves a letter of Cornelius of Rome describing Novatian's ordination irregularity: three simple and rustic bishops from Italy were brought to Rome and compelled to lay hands on Novatian by a counterfeit and vain laying on of hands; Novatian seized the bishopric by craft and treachery. Cornelius also lists the Roman clergy: forty-six presbyters, seven deacons, seven subdeacons, forty-two acolytes, and fifty-two exorcists, readers, and doorkeepers. More than fifteen hundred widows and distressed persons are supported by the Lord's grace and kindness.
notes: Cornelius on Novatian's irregular ordination; Roman church size and clerical orders c. 251

excerpt: At this time, while Origen was conducting catechetical instruction at Alexandria, a deed was done which shows most clearly the immature age at which he was then still to be reckoned. Clement of Alexandria had taught him; and when Clement withdrew, Origen continued the school at the request of Demetrius the bishop.

Paraphrase: Eusebius reports that Stephen of Rome wrote to Cyprian and the African bishops concerning the rebaptism controversy, insisting that no innovation should be made contrary to tradition and that those baptized in heretical communities should receive only the laying on of hands for reconciliation, not rebaptism. He threatened to break communion with the African churches.
notes: Stephen's position: tradition forbids rebaptism; laying on of hands sufficient for reconciliation

excerpt: Paraphrase: Eusebius notes that Firmilian of Caesarea in Cappadocia strongly supported Cyprian's position and wrote a sharp letter (preserved as Ep. 75 in Cyprian's collection) criticizing Stephen's appeal to Peter's chair, arguing that Stephen was in fact the schismatic for threatening to break communion. Dionysius of Alexandria also wrote to Stephen urging moderation and peace.
notes: Firmilian's support of Cyprian; Dionysius mediating; rebaptism controversy geography

excerpt: Paraphrase: Eusebius records that Cyprian was banished under the Emperor Valerian and thereafter summoned back only to be executed. Pontius the deacon, who was with him, wrote an account of his life and death.
notes: Cyprian's exile and martyrdom under Valerian; Pontius as witness and biographer

excerpt: Paraphrase: King Ezana of Aksum dedicates his military victory to the Lord of Heaven Lord of Earth using explicitly Christian formulae replacing earlier pagan invocations of Mahrem.
notes: Archaeological evidence: Ezana's later inscriptions use Christian language confirming his conversion.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paul addresses the churches of Galatia.
Paraphrase: Paul goes up to Jerusalem and presents his gospel; the apostles add nothing and recognize the grace given to him, agreeing that he should go to the Gentiles.
Paraphrase: Meanwhile these islands received the beams of light that is the holy precepts of Christ at the latter part of the reign of Tiberius Caesar.
notes: Gildas 6th-century claim that Christianity reached Britain during Tiberius; late and legendary but influential.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Origen led us first through the disciplines of logic, then natural philosophy, then geometry and astronomy, and finally theology and the study of Scripture; he taught us not by mere lecture but by the example of his life, guiding our souls as a skilled physician guides the sick toward health.
notes: Gregory describes Origen's curriculum and pedagogical method at Caesarea

excerpt: Paraphrase: God brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus.

First of all, believe that God is one, who created all things and set them in order, and brought all things from non-existence into being, who comprehends all things and is alone incomprehensible. Believe Him therefore and fear Him, and in fearing Him be continent.
notes: Opening creedal formula of the Mandates; monotheistic confession as foundation of Christian life

excerpt: I have heard from certain teachers that there is no other repentance except that which took place when we went down into the water and received remission of our former sins. He said: You have heard correctly, for so it is. But I say to you that if anyone is tempted by the devil and sins after that great call, he has one repentance.
notes: Explicit second repentance after baptism; limited but real post-baptismal penance

excerpt: As I was fasting and sitting on a certain mountain, giving thanks to the Lord for all that He had done to me, I see the Shepherd sitting beside me. He said to me: Why have you come here so early? I said: Because, sir, I am keeping a station. What is a station? he asked. I am fasting, sir, I said. What is this fast that you are keeping? he asked. I fast as I have been accustomed, sir, I said. You do not know how to fast to the Lord, he said; this useless fast that you keep for Him is no fast.
notes: Hermas discusses proper fasting; the Shepherd corrects mere ritual fasting; true fasting is moral obedience

excerpt: The Holy Spirit which pre-exists and created all creation, God made to dwell in flesh which He willed. This flesh, therefore, in which the Holy Spirit dwelt, served the Spirit well, walking in holiness and purity, not defiling the Spirit in any way.
notes: The Holy Spirit dwelling in the flesh of Christ; incarnation theology; flesh serving the Spirit in holiness

excerpt: These apostles and teachers preached the name of the Son of God to those who had fallen asleep before them, and they also gave them the seal of the preaching. They descended therefore into the water with them and again came up. But these went down alive and came up alive; but those who had previously fallen asleep went down dead but came up alive. Through them they were made alive and came to know the name of the Son of God.
notes: Baptism as seal necessary for salvation; retroactive sealing of the dead

excerpt: These apostles and teachers who preached the name of the Son of God, after they had fallen asleep in the power of the Son of God, preached to those who had previously fallen asleep, and they gave them the seal of the preaching. They descended therefore into the water with them, and again ascended. For the dead were sealed with the water of life.
notes: Baptism as seal; the dead must receive the water to enter life; baptism required for entry into the tower

excerpt: I obtained mercy because, before I had sinned, I always feared God. There is no repentance for those who have denied the Lord, but for those who have been negligent in their service of the Lord, there is hope of repentance.
notes: Distinction between apostasy (no repentance) and negligence (repentance possible); post-baptismal penance

excerpt: Paraphrase: Those who come to hear the word for the first time are examined as to their manner of life. Catechumens shall hear the word for three years. They shall be examined as to whether they lived piously, honored the widows, visited the sick, and fulfilled every good work.
notes: Three-year catechumenate with moral examination; pre-baptismal instruction period

excerpt: Paraphrase: Let the bishop be ordained, being chosen by all the people. When he has been named and accepted, let the people assemble on the Lord's Day with the presbytery and such bishops as may attend. With the agreement of all, let the bishops lay hands on him.
notes: Earliest known episcopal ordination rite; bishop chosen by the people and ordained by laying on of hands by bishops

excerpt: Paraphrase: At the time appointed for baptism, the bishop shall give thanks over oil and exorcise another oil. The candidates shall renounce Satan. They shall be baptized in the water, the presbyter saying: 'Do you believe in God the Father almighty? Do you believe in Christ Jesus the Son of God? Do you believe in the Holy Spirit in the holy Church?' After each question the candidate is immersed.
notes: Triple-immersion baptism with threefold interrogatory creed; oil of exorcism and oil of thanksgiving; trinitarian structure

excerpt: Paraphrase: After the baptism, the presbyter anoints the baptized with the oil of thanksgiving saying 'I anoint you with holy oil in the name of Jesus Christ.' Then the bishop lays his hand on them, anoints their forehead, and gives them the kiss of peace. From that time they shall pray together with all the people and shall share in the eucharist.
notes: Post-baptismal anointing with chrism; episcopal hand-laying; admission to eucharist; proto-confirmation rite

excerpt: Paraphrase: Every faithful man and woman, when they have risen from sleep in the morning, before they touch any work, shall wash their hands and pray to God. If there is instruction in the word, let each one prefer to go there. Let them pray at the third hour, the sixth hour, the ninth hour, at bedtime, at midnight, and at cockcrow.
notes: Fixed prayer hours throughout the day; daily prayer discipline for the faithful

excerpt: Paraphrase: The eucharistic prayer (anaphora): the bishop gives thanks over the bread and cup, reciting the narrative of institution; the people respond 'Amen'; the deacons present the offering. The prayer includes thanksgiving for creation, incarnation, passion, and resurrection.
notes: Earliest surviving eucharistic prayer; structured anaphora with institution narrative and thanksgiving

excerpt: Paraphrase: Every faithful person shall be careful to receive the eucharist before eating anything else, for if they receive it in faith, even if deadly poison is given them, it will not be able to harm them.
notes: Eucharist received fasting before other food; eucharistic protection language

excerpt: Paraphrase: The presbyter is ordained by the bishop laying hands on him, with the other presbyters also touching him. The deacon is ordained by the bishop alone laying hands on him, because he is not ordained to the priesthood but to the service of the bishop.
notes: Ordination of presbyters and deacons; distinction between priestly and diaconal ordination

excerpt: Paraphrase: The blessed prophets were eyes to us; they foresaw through faith the mysteries of the Word. They ministered to those generations and to us by speaking of things divine. For they did not speak of their own power, but the Word moved them. The prophets first received from God the gift of prophecy; and then they rightly instructed us about the present, past, and future.
notes: Prophetic inspiration from the Word; hermeneutical introduction to eschatological interpretation

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Antichrist will arise as a deceiving king from the tribe of Dan; he will rebuild the temple in Jerusalem; he will persecute the saints; but at the coming of Christ, the Antichrist shall be destroyed and the dead shall be raised — both the righteous to eternal life and the wicked to eternal punishment.
notes: Bodily resurrection at the last day; final judgment; eschatological scenario; Antichrist as persecutor

excerpt: Paraphrase: The first advent of our Lord in the flesh took place in Bethlehem, under Augustus, in the year 5500 from Adam. He suffered in his thirty-third year. And six thousand years must be completed before the second coming, so five hundred years still remain.
notes: Earliest reference to Christ's birth being celebrated on December 25; eschatological chronology; opposes imminentism

excerpt: Paraphrase: Some introduce among us a certain Noetus of Smyrna, who taught that Christ himself was the Father, and that the Father himself was born and suffered and died. They say: Christ was himself the Father and the Father himself suffered.
notes: Introduction of Noetus's modalism/Patripassianism: Father = Son = Christ who suffered

excerpt: Paraphrase: God being alone, and having nothing contemporaneous with himself, determined to create the world. He conceived the world in thought and will. He begat the Word, firstborn of all creation, not as a voice but as the internal reckoning of all things. This was the companion of the Father. The Word was made manifest and became the Son of God.
notes: Logos doctrine against modalism; Word begotten from the Father as distinct person; anti-modalist Logos theology

excerpt: These things then, brethren, are demonstrated by the blessed John and by the Word of truth. Let us therefore glorify God: the Father and the Son, with the Holy Spirit — the holy Trinity. For as the Father is one, his Word is one, and the Spirit from God is one, and one only.
notes: Trinitarian doxology: Father, Son, Holy Spirit; one Trinity; anti-modalist conclusion

excerpt: Paraphrase: Callistus alleged that the parable of the tares growing together with the wheat was spoken in reference to the Church: sinners should remain in the Church. He also affirmed that the ark of Noah was a figure of the Church, in which were dogs, hawks, and other unclean animals — so must the Church tolerate sinners.
notes: Callistus defends leniency toward sinners using parables of tares and Noah's ark; penitential controversy

excerpt: Paraphrase: Zephyrinus was an ignorant and illiterate individual, and one unskilled in the definitions of the Church. Callistus, who was crafty in dealing with evil, and versed in knavery, urged Zephyrinus on, and taught him that the Father and Son are the same. This is the way the wretched man Zephyrinus was beguiled.
notes: Hippolytus accuses Zephyrinus of modalism under Callistus's influence; polemical context

excerpt: Paraphrase: Callistus, having been appointed over the cemetery by Zephyrinus, brought many into his following. He was the first to invent the device of conniving at the sensual pleasures of men, saying that all men had their sins forgiven by himself. For he even permitted women of rank, if they were unmarried and burned with lust, to have a companion of the rank of slave or freedman.
notes: Hippolytus attacks Callistus for moral laxity; permitting marriages between social classes; forgiving grave sins

excerpt: Take heed, then, often to come together to give thanks to God and show forth His praise. For when you assemble frequently in the same place, the powers of Satan are destroyed.
notes: Exhortation to frequent eucharistic assembly

excerpt: Paraphrase: Our God Jesus Christ was conceived in the womb by Mary of the seed of David but by the Holy Ghost; the virginity of Mary was hidden from the prince of this world as one of three great mysteries wrought in silence.

Obey the bishop and presbytery with undivided mind, breaking one and the same bread, which is the medicine of immortality and the antidote to prevent us from dying but which causes that we should live forever in Jesus Christ.
notes: Eucharist as medicine of immortality

excerpt: There is one Physician who is possessed both of flesh and spirit; both made and not made; God existing in flesh; true life in death; both of Mary and of God; first possible and then impossible — even Jesus Christ our Lord.
notes: Christ as physician both fleshly and spiritual; anti-docetist double nature; virgin birth implied

excerpt: Study to be established in the doctrines of the Lord and the apostles, that so all things, whatsoever you do, may prosper both in flesh and spirit, in faith and love, in the Son and in the Father and in the Holy Ghost, in the beginning and at the end; with your most admirable bishop and the finely woven spiritual crown of your presbytery and the deacons.
notes: Apostolic doctrine received and guarded by bishop with presbytery and deacons; Trinitarian grounding

excerpt: I exhort you to study to do all things with a divine harmony, while your bishop presides in the place of God, and your presbyters in the place of the assembly of the apostles, along with your deacons who are most dear to me and are entrusted with the service of Jesus Christ.
notes: Bishop as representative of God; threefold ministry with explicit apostolic correspondence

excerpt: Be careful therefore to have but one Eucharist. For there is one flesh of our Lord Jesus Christ and one cup to the unity of His blood, one altar, as there is one bishop along with the presbytery and deacons.
notes: One Eucharist one bishop one altar; episcopal unity of eucharistic celebration

excerpt: I no longer take pleasure in perishable food or in the delights of this world. I desire the bread of God, which is the flesh of Jesus Christ who was of the seed of David; and for drink I desire His blood, which is love incorruptible.
notes: Eucharist as bread of God and flesh of Christ; vivid real-presence language

excerpt: Paraphrase: Ignatius confesses that Christ was truly of the seed of David, truly born of a virgin, baptized by John that all righteousness might be fulfilled, truly crucified under Pontius Pilate.
They abstain from Eucharist and prayer because they confess not the Eucharist to be the flesh of our Saviour Jesus Christ which suffered for our sins and which the Father raised.
notes: Anti-docetist eucharistic realism, explicit flesh language

excerpt: See that you all follow the bishop. Let that be deemed a proper Eucharist which is administered either by the bishop or by one to whom he has entrusted it. It is not lawful without the bishop either to baptize or to celebrate.
notes: Episcopal control over eucharist and baptism

excerpt: In like manner, let all reverence the deacons as an appointment of Jesus Christ, and the bishop as Jesus Christ who is the Son of the Father, and the presbyters as the sanhedrin of God and assembly of the apostles. Apart from these, there is no church.
notes: Threefold ministry required for valid church; bishop as Christ, presbyters as apostles

excerpt: He that is within the altar is pure, but he that is outside is not pure; that is, he who does anything apart from the bishop and presbytery and deacons is not pure in his conscience.
notes: Purity of worship defined by communion with bishop; episcopal authority extended to all liturgical action

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Church dispersed throughout the whole world received from the apostles this faith: in one God the Father Almighty; one Christ Jesus the Son of God who became incarnate for our salvation; and the Holy Spirit who proclaimed through the prophets the birth from a virgin, passion, resurrection from the dead, ascension into heaven, and future gathering of all things and resurrection of all flesh.

Paraphrase: The Church, though scattered throughout Germany, Spain, Gaul, the East, Egypt, Libya, and the central regions, preserves this preaching with one soul and one heart; no ruler in the Churches teaches anything different; the faith is neither added to by the eloquent nor diminished by the slow.

Valentinus adapted the principles of the heretical school called Gnostic to his own peculiar character of school. He affirmed that in the invisible and ineffable heights there exists a perfect, pre-existent Aeon — Proarche, Propator, and Bythus — invisible and incomprehensible. With him was also Ennoia, whom he calls Charis and Sige. This Bythus, at last resolving to send forth from himself the beginning of all things, deposited this production as a seed in the womb of Sige. The result was Nous and Aletheia — the beginning of a vast pleroma of divine emanations.

Simon the Samaritan was that magician of whom Luke, the disciple and follower of the apostles, writes in Acts. He claimed to be the supreme divine Power, the father of all heresy. Having purchased a certain woman named Helena, he declared her to be the first Conception of his mind, the mother of all, through whom he had originally conceived the creation of angels and archangels.

Basilides says that there is one unbegotten father, by name Abraxas, by whom was born Nous; from Nous, Logos; from Logos, Phronesis; from Phronesis, Sophia and Dynamis; and from these the rulers, angels, and first heaven. He affirmed that Jesus did not suffer death, but that Simon of Cyrene was compelled to carry his cross for him, and was crucified in his stead — transfigured so as to appear to be Jesus. But Jesus himself received the form of Simon and, standing by, laughed at those who crucified him.

A certain Cerinthus in Asia taught that the world was not made by the primary God, but by a Power far separated from him and ignorant of the supreme authority. He maintained that Jesus was not born of a virgin, but was the son of Joseph and Mary according to the ordinary course of human generation. After baptism, Christ descended upon him in the form of a dove from the Supreme Ruler; then he proclaimed the unknown Father and performed miracles. But at last Christ departed from Jesus, and then Jesus suffered and rose again.

Those called Ebionites agree that the world was made by God; but their opinions with respect to the Lord are similar to those of Cerinthus and Carpocrates. They use the Gospel according to Matthew only, and repudiate the Apostle Paul, maintaining that he was an apostate from the law. As regards the prophetical writings, they endeavour to interpret them in a singular manner. They practise circumcision and observe the Mosaic law. They affirm Jesus was the son of Joseph and Mary.

Marcion of Pontus succeeded [Cerdo] and developed his doctrine, blaspheming with shameless effrontery Him proclaimed as God by the law and the prophets, declaring Him the author of evils, taking delight in war, infirm of purpose, and contrary to Himself. But Jesus, derived from that father above the God who made the world, came into Judaea in the times of Pontius Pilate, was manifested in the form of a man — abolishing the prophets and the law — but not truly taking flesh. He also mutilated the Gospel according to Luke, removing all that is written respecting the generation of the Lord.

Saturninus, who flourished at Antioch, set forth one father unknown to all, who made angels, archangels, powers, and potentates. The world was made by a company of seven angels. The Saviour he averred was unborn, incorporeal, and without form, but supposed to be a man in appearance only. The God of the Jews, he declared, was one of the angels. Marriage and generation, he maintained, are of Satan.

He came to save all through means of himself — all, I say, who through him are born again to God, infants, and children, and boys, and youths, and old men. He therefore passed through every age, becoming an infant for infants, sanctifying infants.
notes: Christ sanctifying every age including infancy; context for early infant baptism reflection

excerpt: Paraphrase: We learned the plan of salvation from those through whom the Gospel came to us. Matthew issued a written Gospel among the Hebrews while Peter and Paul were preaching at Rome. Mark, the disciple and interpreter of Peter, handed down what Peter had preached. Luke, Paul's companion, recorded his Gospel. Afterwards John, who leaned on the Lord's breast, published his Gospel during his residence at Ephesus.

This Spirit did descend upon the Lord as a dove; upon us also the same Spirit descended when we were baptized. And just as He who was born of a virgin became Christ, so we too, by birth through the Spirit, become sons of God and fellow-heirs with Christ.
notes: Baptism gives the Holy Spirit; the baptism of believers parallels the anointing of Christ

excerpt: Paraphrase: When confuted from the Scriptures, heretics accuse them of being incorrect or ambiguous and claim truth was delivered by word of mouth alone; when referred to the apostolic tradition preserved by the succession of presbyters in the Churches, they object to that too — they consent neither to Scripture nor to tradition.

Paraphrase: It is within the power of all in every Church to contemplate the tradition of the apostles manifested throughout the whole world and to reckon up those who were instituted bishops by the apostles and their succession to our own times; those who taught nothing like what the heretics rave about.

Paraphrase: The tradition derived from the apostles of the very great, very ancient, universally known Church founded at Rome by Peter and Paul — every Church must agree with this Church on account of its preeminent authority (potiorem principalitatem); the faith preached to men comes down to our time by means of the successions of the bishops.

Paraphrase: The Rome bishop succession: Peter and Paul committed the episcopate to Linus; then Anacletus; then Clement (third from the apostles, who had seen the blessed apostles); then Evaristus, Alexander, Sixtus, Telephorus (gloriously martyred), Hyginus, Pius, Anicetus, Soter, and Eleutherius (twelfth from the apostles, now in office). In this order and by this succession the ecclesiastical tradition from the apostles and the preaching of the truth have come down to us.

Paraphrase: Polycarp was instructed by apostles, conversed with many who had seen Christ, and was appointed bishop of Smyrna by apostles in Asia; Irenaeus saw him in his early youth. Polycarp came to Rome in the time of Anicetus and caused many to turn from heretics to the Church, proclaiming the one truth received from the apostles. He called Marcion the first-born of Satan.

Paraphrase: The apostles lodged in the Church all things pertaining to truth as in a rich bank; she is the entrance to life, all others are thieves and robbers. In any dispute one should have recourse to the most ancient Churches where the apostles held constant intercourse.

Paraphrase: Valentinus came to Rome in the time of Hyginus; Cerdon, Marcion's predecessor, also came in the time of Hyginus; Marcion himself flourished under Anicetus. All these heretical teachers arose after and post-date the apostolic Church; none can trace their origin to the apostles.
