Paraphrase: The oblation of the Church is accounted a pure sacrifice acceptable to God throughout all the world — not that He needs it, but the offerer is glorified in what he offers. The class of oblations has not been set aside; sacrifices existed among the Jews and exist now in the Church; only the species has changed, the offering now made not by slaves but by freemen giving first-fruits.

For in the Church God has set apostles, prophets, teachers, and all the other means through which the Spirit works; of which all those are not partakers who do not join themselves to the Church, but defraud themselves of life through their perverse opinions and infamous behavior. For where the Church is, there is the Spirit of God; and where the Spirit of God is, there is the Church, and every kind of grace.
notes: The Church everywhere possesses the Spirit; the Spirit is where the Church is; outside the Church no life

excerpt: Paraphrase: If the flesh cannot be saved then the Lord did not redeem us with His blood; but the cup of the Eucharist is the communion of His blood and the bread the communion of His body. The bread and cup, receiving the Word of God, become the body and blood of Christ by which our flesh is nourished; and our bodies, nourished by the eucharist and deposited in the earth, shall rise at their appointed time, the Word of God granting them resurrection to the glory of God the Father.

Irenaeus cites Papias: The days will come in which vines shall grow, each having ten thousand branches, each branch having ten thousand twigs, each twig ten thousand shoots, and in each shoot ten thousand clusters. Papias indicates in writing that these things will take place in the time of the kingdom and that they are credible to those who believe them.
notes: Irenaeus cites Papias for millenarian bodily resurrection hope; confirms Papias learned this from elders who saw John

excerpt: Paraphrase: James calls hearers to draw near to God, cleanse their hands, humble themselves, and repent before the Lord.
Paraphrase: John testifies about Jesus at Bethany beyond the Jordan where he was baptizing.
Paraphrase: Jesus calls Philip, who brings Nathanael, who confesses him.
Paraphrase: Lazarus, Martha, and Mary are introduced at Bethany near Jerusalem.
Paraphrase: Judas arrives with officers, Jesus is arrested in the garden, and he restrains violent resistance.
Paraphrase: Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus bury Jesus.
Paraphrase: Mary Magdalene comes to the tomb and encounters the risen Jesus.
Paraphrase: These things are written so that you may believe Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.
Paraphrase: Josephus describes John as a good man exhorting virtue and baptism and says Herod slew him.
Paraphrase: Josephus identifies Pontius Pilate as prefect governing Judea.
Paraphrase: Josephus says the high priest Ananus assembled the Sanhedrin, accused James the brother of Jesus called Christ and some others, and delivered them to be stoned.
Paraphrase: Josephus describes the Pharisees, Sadducees, and Essenes as distinct schools among the Jews and gives an extended portrait of the Essenes.
Paraphrase: Josephus portrays the Zealots as a violent faction dominating Jerusalem during the war.
Paraphrase: Josephus narrates the burning of the temple and the destruction of Jerusalem under the Roman assault.
Paraphrase: After judgment the Lord will heal his servants and the righteous will see great peace.
Paraphrase: The Lord comes with holy myriads to execute judgment against all the ungodly.

Paraphrase: Christians worship the Maker of the universe by prayer and thanksgiving without material sacrifice; Jesus Christ is the Son of the true God in the second place; the prophetic Spirit is in the third place.
Paraphrase: The Word who is the first-offspring of God was produced without sexual union and became Jesus Christ our Teacher, who was crucified, died, rose again, and ascended into heaven.

Paraphrase: Isaiah foretold the virgin birth; Mary the virgin of the race of David received faith and joy when the angel Gabriel announced the glad tidings to her; the Holy Spirit came upon her and the power of the Most High overshadowed her.

Paraphrase: Those who lived according to the Logos are Christians even if thought atheists — Socrates and Heraclitus among them; Christ is the first-begotten of God and the Logos of whom every race of men were partakers.

Paraphrase: Among the Greeks the Logos condemned false gods through Socrates; but among the Barbarians the Logos Himself took shape and became man, Jesus Christ; we worship the Father, the Son who came forth from Him and taught us, and the prophetic Spirit.

Those who believe and undertake to live accordingly are brought where there is water and regenerated in the name of God the Father, our Saviour Jesus Christ, and the Holy Spirit. This washing is called illumination.
notes: Detailed baptism description with trinitarian formula

excerpt: After baptism we bring the person to the assembly for prayers. Bread and cup of wine mixed with water are brought to the president who gives thanks. This food is called Eucharist, of which only baptized may partake. Not as common bread and drink do we receive these, but as the flesh and blood of Jesus who was made flesh.
notes: Full description of post-baptismal eucharist with real-presence language

excerpt: On the day called Sunday all gather to one place. Memoirs of apostles or writings of prophets are read, the president instructs, then all pray together. Bread and wine with water are brought, the president offers prayers and thanks, the people say Amen, and there is distribution to each.
notes: Sunday assembly pattern with eucharist description

excerpt: God speaks by Malachi: I have no pleasure in you, and I will not accept your sacrifices; for from the rising to the setting of the sun, my name has been glorified among the Gentiles, and in every place incense is offered to my name, and a pure offering. Now concerning the sacrifices which you at that time offered, He speaks; but concerning those which are offered by us in every place through this Eucharist, He prophesied.
notes: Justin's fullest statement that the Eucharist is the pure sacrifice predicted by Malachi in every place

excerpt: The offering of fine flour commanded for those cleansed from leprosy was a type of the bread of the Eucharist, which Jesus Christ commanded to be made in remembrance of his suffering; and the offering of bread and cup was also prophesied by Malachi.
notes: Eucharist as fulfillment of OT type and Malachi's prophesy; sacrifice language

excerpt: Therefore the Holy Spirit proclaimed beforehand by Isaiah the great mystery of how the Lord would be born of a virgin, and it says: Behold, a virgin shall conceive and bear a son. It is clear that Isaiah said the Christ would be born of a virgin.
notes: Explicit use of Isaiah 7:14 for the virgin birth of Christ against Jewish objections

locator: Book of Laws of Countries (central argument)
excerpt: Paraphrase: Bardaisan catalogs the diverse laws and customs of nations — Brahmins in India, Persians, Britons, Chinese — demonstrating that human behavior differs by culture, not by zodiac; if fate determined all, customs would be uniform; therefore free will prevails over astral determinism.
notes: Diversity of customs disproves fate

excerpt: Paraphrase: Bardaisan argues that human beings possess free will (heruta) given by God; fate and the stars influence the body and nature, but the soul's rational choices are free; God is good and created humans with the capacity to choose between good and evil.
notes: Free will argument

excerpt: Paraphrase: Jesus passes through Jericho and speaks with Zacchaeus.
Paraphrase: Judas approaches Jesus with the crowd, Jesus is seized, and he speaks about their coming with swords and clubs.
Paraphrase: Jesus shares the Passover meal with his disciples, gives the bread and cup, and speaks of betrayal.
Paraphrase: The risen Christ says repentance and forgiveness are to be proclaimed in his name.
Paraphrase: The messengers remind the women that the Son of Man must be crucified and on the third day rise.
Paraphrase: Jesus leads the disciples out toward Bethany, blesses them, and is carried up into heaven.
Paraphrase: John appears in the region around the Jordan proclaiming a baptism of repentance.
Paraphrase: Jesus comes to Nazareth, where he had been brought up, and enters the synagogue on the Sabbath.
Paraphrase: Women including Mary Magdalene accompany Jesus in the Galilean ministry.
Paraphrase: Jesus comes into Galilee proclaiming God kingdom and calling for repentance and belief.
Paraphrase: Jesus calls Simon, Andrew, James, and John, and they leave their work to follow him.
Paraphrase: Jesus approaches Jerusalem, rides in with acclamation, and enters the city and temple precincts.
Paraphrase: Jesus enters the temple precincts in Jerusalem and disrupts buying and selling.
Paraphrase: Pharisees and Herodians question Jesus in Jerusalem about paying taxes to Caesar.
Paraphrase: Jesus is handed over to Pilate, examined, and condemned to be crucified.
Paraphrase: Jesus is crucified, mocked, and dies, and the centurion witnesses the manner of his death.
Paraphrase: The young man announces that Jesus has been raised and is not in the tomb.
Paraphrase: Pharisees go out and immediately conspire with the Herodians against Jesus after the Sabbath controversy.
Paraphrase: Herod imprisons John, orders his death, and John's disciples lay his body in a tomb.

Lord God Almighty, Father of your beloved and blessed Servant Jesus Christ, through whom we have received full knowledge of you… I bless you, I glorify you, through the eternal High Priest Jesus Christ, your beloved Servant, through whom be glory to you, with him and the Holy Spirit, both now and forever.
notes: Polycarp's pre-martyrdom prayer; Trinitarian doxology; Christ as eternal high priest

excerpt: The Smyrnaeans wished to take the body from the fire; but the jealous evil one, knowing that the centurion had guarded it from Nicetes, suggested to him to burn the body. Afterwards we took up his bones, more precious than precious stones and finer than refined gold, and deposited them in a suitable place.
notes: Bones of Polycarp gathered and treated as precious; annual commemoration described

excerpt: O Lord God Almighty, Father of your beloved and blessed Servant Jesus Christ, I give you thanks that you have counted me worthy of this day and this hour, that I should have a part in the number of your martyrs, in the cup of your Christ. I bless you and glorify you through the eternal and heavenly High Priest, Jesus Christ your beloved Servant.
notes: Polycarp's pre-death prayer; eucharistic cup language; sacrifice and priesthood resonance

excerpt: Paraphrase: Many desired to possess Polycarp's holy flesh; the church gathered his bones, more precious than jewels, deposited them in a fitting place, and resolved to celebrate the anniversary of his martyrdom each year.

When Polycarp entered the stadium there came a voice from heaven saying: Be strong, Polycarp, and play the man. The proconsul asked: Are you Polycarp? He replied: I am. The proconsul urged: Take the oath and I will release you. Polycarp said: Eighty-six years have I served Him and He never did me any wrong. How can I blaspheme my King who saved me?
notes: Polycarp's witness before magistrates; identifies his 86-year discipleship

excerpt: Paraphrase: Matthew lists the names of the Twelve apostles including Thomas, Matthew, Philip, and Bartholomew.
Paraphrase: The angel tells the women that Jesus has been raised from the dead and will go before the disciples into Galilee.
Paraphrase: Jesus begins proclaiming, Repent, for the kingdom of heaven has drawn near.
Paraphrase: Jesus goes throughout Galilee teaching, proclaiming the kingdom, and healing.
Paraphrase: The crowds recognize that Jesus teaches with authority unlike the scribes.

Paraphrase: Christ is the Passover lamb who was slain in Abel's place, bound in Isaac's place, exiled in Jacob's place, sold in Joseph's place, and offered as the lamb; this is the one who was crucified and hanged on the tree and buried, and who rose from the dead.

Paraphrase: He who formed man from the earth was born of a virgin; he who suspended the earth in space was himself suspended on a tree; he who fixed the heavens was nailed to the cross; he who was set in a tomb rose from the dead and lifted up the voice to announce glad tidings to the dead.

The rule of truth requires that we should first of all things believe in God the Father and Lord Almighty, that is, the most perfect Creator of all things, who has hung the heavens in lofty sublimity, has laid the solid foundations of the earth, has spread out the seas, and has distributed all things with their fitting ornaments.
notes: Rule of faith opening; God the Father as Creator; creedal structure

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Holy Spirit is the same who was promised by the prophets and poured out on the apostles. He is the sanctifier of the faith of those who believe in the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. He is the same Spirit who worked in the prophets, who was afterward breathed upon the apostles, and who is the bond of the unity of the Church.
notes: Holy Spirit as sanctifier; trinitarian faith; Spirit's role in Church unity; pneumatology

excerpt: Paraphrase: If we consider the teaching of the whole of Scripture, we must necessarily conclude that the Father is God, that the Son is God, and that the Holy Spirit is God; and yet they are not three Gods but one God. The substance is one but the persons are distinct. He proceeds from the Father, as the Son also himself testifies.
notes: One God in three persons; distinct persons, one substance; Latin trinitarian synthesis

excerpt: Paraphrase: Scripture teaches that the Son of God, the Word, was with the Father before the creation of the world. By him all things were made. He is the same who was foretold by the prophets, who took upon himself our flesh, who was truly born of a virgin, who was truly crucified, who truly died, who truly rose again.
notes: Logos pre-existence; true incarnation; real suffering and death; bodily resurrection; anti-docetist

excerpt: The Son, who is the Word, was in the beginning with God, and the Word was God. This Word is not, as some suppose, an impersonal attribute of God but is a living, subsistent being — the very Wisdom and Truth of the Father, eternally generated from him.
notes: Logos as a subsistent divine person, eternally generated

excerpt: Paraphrase: The deeper meaning of Scripture is found by those who seek the spiritual sense beneath the letter; the Gospel of John especially leads the reader from the visible to the invisible, from the bodily narrative to the spiritual reality of the Logos.
notes: Allegorical exegesis applied to the Gospel of John

excerpt: Paraphrase: The bread which God the Word declares to be his body is the nourishing Word of truth; the cup which he calls his blood is the Word which gives drink and inebriates the hearts of those who drink it; for the body and blood of the Word of God can only be the words of Scripture.
notes: Origen's spiritualized eucharistic interpretation — Word as true food

excerpt: Paraphrase: On every bishop and presbyter rests the obligation to teach correctly and feed the flock; those who fail in this duty will be held accountable; the bishop must hold fast to the word of God and guard apostolic tradition.
notes: Episcopal responsibility for teaching and guarding apostolic tradition

excerpt: Paraphrase: Justification comes through faith, not through works of the law; yet this faith, once received, must be active in love and in the exercise of free will toward the good; grace and free will cooperate in the work of salvation.
notes: Justification by faith active in love; grace and free will cooperate

excerpt: The Church received from the apostles the tradition of giving baptism even to infants. For those to whom the secrets of the divine mysteries were committed knew that there is in everyone the innate stains of sin, which must be washed away through water and the Spirit.
notes: Infant baptism as apostolic tradition — the most explicit early patristic statement

excerpt: Paraphrase: Baptism is a death and burial with Christ; rising from the baptismal water signifies new life; the one baptized is regenerated, freed from the dominion of sin, and incorporated into the body of Christ.
notes: Baptismal regeneration — death, burial, and new life in Christ

excerpt: Jesus was truly born of a virgin according to the promises of the prophets; the Spirit of God came upon the virgin, and the power of the Most High overshadowed her, and what was born of her was called the Son of God.
notes: Virgin birth defended against Celsus's mockery

excerpt: But we believe that the whole creation looks forward to liberation from the bondage of corruption, and that the body which has been sown in corruption will be raised in incorruption. For it is not a different body that is raised, but the same body purified.
notes: Bodily resurrection defended — the same body raised, purified

excerpt: Christians are taught to accept the Scriptures of the Old Testament as the oracles of God, and to regard them as containing shadows and images of divine things; the law is spiritual, and the prophets spoke by the Spirit of God.
notes: OT authority and spiritual interpretation defended against pagan attack

excerpt: We offer to the Creator of all things prayers through the one high priest, and we offer our supplications to the Word of God. To him we offer our every prayer, petition, thanksgiving, and intercession. And let none fear that he is praying to two Gods.
notes: Trinitarian prayer — prayer offered to the Father through the Son

excerpt: The Son is the brightness of the eternal light. If this is so, then the Son is eternally begotten. For as brightness is generated from light without any interval, so also the Son is generated from the Father without any interval.
notes: Eternal generation illustrated by the analogy of light and its brightness

excerpt: Let him who assigns a beginning to the Word or Wisdom of God take care that he be not guilty of impiety against the unbegotten Father, seeing that he denies that he has always been a Father, and has always generated his Word, and has always had his Wisdom.
notes: Eternal generation of the Son — there was never when the Father was without the Son

excerpt: Paraphrase: The end is always like the beginning; as there was one beginning of all things, so there must be one end of all things; the diverse and varying conditions of rational beings will be restored to that original harmony through the goodness of God and the subjection of all things to Christ.
notes: Universal restoration (apokatastasis) — all rational beings eventually restored

excerpt: Paraphrase: Souls pre-existed before they were placed in bodies; their different conditions in the present life result from their varying use of free will in the prior spiritual state; some fell further from God and became demons, others became angels, while humans occupy a middle state.
notes: Pre-existence of souls and the explanation of different conditions through prior free will

excerpt: Since the teaching of the Church includes the doctrine that every rational soul is possessed of free will and volition; and since we must demonstrate that neither by any compulsion from God nor from any other cause is any one forced either to good or evil; let us now examine this.
notes: Free will as foundational church teaching; rational souls possess genuine freedom

excerpt: The way to understand the Scriptures is by recognizing that they have a threefold meaning: the bodily sense, the soul sense, and the spiritual sense, corresponding to the body, soul, and spirit of a person.
notes: Threefold sense of Scripture — literal, moral, spiritual/allegorical

excerpt: The following are the particular points which are clearly delivered in the teaching of the apostles: First, that there is one God who created and arranged all things; second, that Jesus Christ was begotten of the Father before all creatures, and after he had ministered to the Father in the creation of all things, in the last times emptied himself and was made man; third, that the Holy Spirit was associated in honour and dignity with the Father and the Son.
notes: Origen's rule of faith in the Preface to De Principiis; trinitarian formula and apostolic tradition

excerpt: Paraphrase: The martyrs follow Christ in his suffering and share in his passion; their blood is a seed of future Christians; by their testimony they confess Christ before the powers of this world and receive the crown of life promised by the Lord.
notes: Martyrdom as witness to Christ and participation in his passion

excerpt: Paraphrase: God created rational beings with free will so that goodness might be their own, not forced; the one who chooses martyrdom does so freely, and this freedom is what makes the act meritorious; no fate or necessity compels the choice — it is the soul's free offering to God.
notes: Free will as basis for merit of martyrdom

excerpt: Paraphrase: When did the land of the Britons before the coming of Christ agree upon the worship of one God?
notes: Origen's rhetorical question mentioning British pre-Christian paganism implying Christianity had since reached there.

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Scripture has a body, a soul, and a spirit; the body is the literal meaning which the simple grasp; the soul is the moral application which strengthens conduct; the spirit is the hidden divine mystery which only the mature and spiritual comprehend through allegory.
notes: Threefold sense of Scripture restated — body, soul, spirit

excerpt: Paraphrase: God does not desire the destruction of sinners but their repentance; even after baptism, if a person falls, there remains the hard and painful road of penance — tears, fasting, and public confession before the bishop — by which the sinner may be restored; the Church has the power of the keys to bind and loose.
notes: Post-baptismal penance through tears, fasting, confession; Church holds power of keys

excerpt: Paraphrase: The sacrifices of the old law are types and shadows of the true sacrifice which is Christ; the Levitical priesthood prefigures the priesthood of Christ and his church; the offerings of the altar now are the prayers and spiritual sacrifices of Christians presented through the one high priest, Christ.
notes: Levitical sacrifices as types of Christ's sacrifice and the church's spiritual offering

excerpt: Paraphrase: There are various remissions of sins spoken of in Scripture: baptism as the first, martyrdom as the second, almsgiving as the third, forgiving others as the fourth, converting a sinner as the fifth, abundant charity as the sixth, and penance through tears and affliction as the seventh.
notes: Seven ways of remission of sins — penance theology

excerpt: Paraphrase: Ordination to the priesthood is conferred by the laying on of hands, as Moses laid hands on Joshua and as the apostles laid hands on those they appointed; the bishop who ordains must be worthy, and the one ordained must be examined and approved by the people.
notes: Ordination by laying on of hands; continuity from Moses through apostles to bishops

excerpt: Let us inquire why infants are baptized. If there were nothing in infants which required the remission of sins and the grace of baptism, the grace of baptism would seem superfluous.
notes: Infant baptism presupposed — why baptize infants if no original sin?

excerpt: Mary remained a virgin. For it was fitting that he who came into the world to restore purity to human nature should be born of a pure virgin. The brothers of Jesus mentioned in Scripture are sons of Joseph by a former wife, not children of Mary.
notes: Perpetual virginity of Mary — brothers of Jesus are Joseph's sons from prior marriage

excerpt: Paraphrase: The words 'Our Father who art in heaven' teach us that God is not confined to a place but is present everywhere; 'give us this day our daily bread' refers to the substantial bread, the Word of God, which nourishes the soul; we must pray always and without ceasing, making the whole of life a single unbroken prayer.
notes: Continual prayer — the whole of life made into unceasing prayer; Lord's Prayer exegesis

excerpt: Paraphrase: Prayer must be addressed to the Father through the Son in the Holy Spirit; the Son is our high priest and intercessor, not himself the ultimate object of prayer but the way to the Father; the Holy Spirit intercedes for us with sighs too deep for words.
notes: Trinitarian structure of prayer — to Father, through Son, in Spirit

excerpt: Paraphrase: Papias says he would not hesitate to expound along with the interpretations whatever he had ever learned from the elders and preserved in memory. He inquired about what the elders said — what Andrew or Peter said, what Philip or Thomas or James, what John or Matthew or any of the Lord's disciples.
notes: Papias deliberately prefers oral tradition from those who knew the apostles; evidence for tradition-reception chain

excerpt: Matthew composed the oracles (logia) in the Hebrew language, and each person interpreted them as best he could.
notes: Papias attests Matthew's Hebrew logia; key text for apostolic authorship and canonical tradition

excerpt: These things are credible to believers according to Papias the disciple of John, who was a companion of Polycarp, as he recorded in his fourth book; for there were five books composed by him.
notes: Irenaeus names Papias as disciple of John and companion of Polycarp; connects succession chain Apostle→Papias→Polycarp→Irenaeus

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paul and Timothy address all the saints in Christ Jesus who are in Philippi, with the overseers and deacons.
Paraphrase: Paul calls Epaphroditus his brother, coworker, and fellow soldier sent by the Philippians.
Paraphrase: Euodia and Syntyche labored side by side with Paul in the gospel.
Paraphrase: Paul addresses Philemon, Apphia, Archippus, and the church in their house.

Paraphrase: Theophilus called the Indian was sent by Emperor Constantius on an embassy to the Himyarites in south Arabia. He was originally from the island of Dibus (Socotra). He converted the Himyarites and built three churches: one in Zafar the capital, one in Aden, and one near the Persian Gulf. He also visited the Aksumites and corrected their worship.
notes: Key source for Arabian/Himyarite Christianity and Theophilus's mission; preserved in Photius's epitome.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Theophilus proceeded from the Himyarites to his own country where he found Christians who had learned of Christ from the apostle Bartholomew; they held to the doctrine of other in substance.
notes: Reports pre-existing Indian Christians with heterodox Christology; important for Thomas/Bartholomew tradition.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Paul names Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke as fellow workers.

Who raised him from the dead, and gave him glory, and a throne at His right hand. To Him are all things in heaven and on earth subjected; Whom every spirit serves; Who comes as the Judge of living and dead; Whose blood God will require of them that are disobedient to Him.
notes: Resurrection, judgment, and lordship of Christ; creedal summary

excerpt: Believing in him who raised our Lord Jesus Christ from the dead and gave him glory and a throne at his right hand: to him every thing in heaven and on earth is subject, every spirit serves him. He is coming as judge of the living and the dead. His blood will God require of those who do not believe in him.
notes: Resurrection and lordship of Christ; apostolic tradition received and transmitted

excerpt: The deacons must be blameless before the face of His righteousness as deacons of God and Christ. The presbyters must be compassionate, merciful to all, turning back those who have gone astray, caring for all the weak, neglecting neither widow nor orphan.
notes: Distinct roles of deacons and presbyters; clear threefold-order presupposition

excerpt: For every one who shall not confess that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh, is antichrist; and whosoever shall not confess the testimony of the Cross, is of the devil.
notes: Explicit anti-docetist test: confession of Christ come in the flesh is the mark of orthodoxy; 1 John 4:2 applied

excerpt: For whosoever does not confess that Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is antichrist; and whoever does not confess the testimony of the cross is of the devil; and whosoever shall pervert the oracles of the Lord to his own lusts and say there is neither resurrection nor judgment, he is the firstborn of Satan.
notes: Anti-docetist confession of incarnation and resurrection as received rule of faith

excerpt: Paraphrase: The Lord Messiah judges the nations and purifies Jerusalem.
Paraphrase: John is told to write in a book and send it to the seven churches in Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea.
Paraphrase: John says that he was on the island called Patmos because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.
Paraphrase: The believers in Pergamum hold fast Christ's name even in the days when Antipas, his faithful witness, was killed among them.
Paraphrase: In Pergamum some hold the teaching of the Nicolaitans.
Paraphrase: The Ephesian church is commended for hating the works of the Nicolaitans, which Christ also hates.
Paraphrase: Paul addresses all God beloved in Rome who are called to be saints.
Paraphrase: The confession of faith centers on God raising Jesus from the dead.
Paraphrase: Paul commends Phoebe to the Roman believers.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Tryphaena, Tryphosa, and Persis, women who labored much in the Lord.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Rufus, chosen in the Lord, and Rufus's mother.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers with them.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Philologus, Julia, Nereus and his sister, Olympas, and all the saints with them.
Paraphrase: Gaius and Erastus, the city treasurer, send greetings with the Roman letter.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Prisca and Aquila, his fellow workers in Christ Jesus.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Prisca and Aquila and also the church in their house.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Mary, who has worked very hard among the Roman believers.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Andronicus and Junia, his kinsmen and fellow prisoners, who were in Christ before him.
Paraphrase: Paul greets Urbanus, a fellow worker in Christ, and Stachys, his beloved.
Paraphrase: The Indian philosopher Metrodorus had earlier traveled to farther India and returned telling of those regions. The gospel had reached these parts through earlier apostolic mission.
notes: Brief notice on earlier Indian Christian presence; context for Pantaenus and Thomas traditions.

excerpt: Paraphrase: A philosopher named Meropius of Tyre sailed to India with two boys Frumentius and Aedesius. The ship was seized and all killed except the boys who were brought to the king of Aksum. When the king died the queen asked Frumentius to help govern. Frumentius sought out Roman traders who were Christians and built churches. Later he traveled to Alexandria where Athanasius consecrated him bishop. He returned to Aksum and converted many including the king.
notes: Primary Western source for the christianization of Aksum; Rufinus heard the account from Aedesius himself.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Do not delay turning to the Lord and do not postpone repentance.

Paraphrase: Socrates narrates how Frumentius, shipwrecked in Ethiopia, gained the king's trust, introduced Christianity, and was later consecrated bishop by Athanasius of Alexandria — an independent Greek confirmation of Rufinus's Latin account.
notes: Socrates Scholasticus HE 1.19; NPNF2 translation.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tacitus narrates the Great Fire of Rome and Nero's punishment of Christians to suppress the rumor against himself.

We do not speak nonsense when we say the body will rise again. The dissolution of matter does not mean annihilation. God who is almighty is able to raise it. Fire dissolves material things, but God who made them from non-existence can restore them from dissolution.
notes: Bodily resurrection affirmed; Creator God as guarantee of resurrection; anti-materialist argument

excerpt: We are not fools, men of Greece, nor are we uttering nonsense when we report that God was born in the form of man. Compare your own stories: the gods of the Greeks changed into birds and beasts and flowers. God in a human body is far more decorous.
notes: Christ's incarnation defended against pagan objection; anti-docetist realism assumed

excerpt: God is a Spirit, not pervading matter, but the maker of material spirits and of the forms that are in matter; he is invisible, impalpable, being himself the Father of both sensible and invisible things. Him do we know from his creation, and apprehend his invisible power by his works.
notes: God as Spirit beyond matter; Logos theology background; Creator God affirmed

excerpt: Paraphrase: In whom else have all nations believed but in Christ who has already come? Parthians, Medes, Elamites, those in Mesopotamia, Armenia, Phrygia, Cappadocia, and the haunts of the Britons inaccessible to the Romans but subjugated to Christ.
notes: Tertullian's rhetorical list of peoples reached by Christ including Britannia beyond Roman access.

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian introduces the refutation of Marcion's two-God theory, defending the identity of the Creator with the God of the gospel and the authority of the Old Testament as Christian Scripture.
notes: Introduces the fundamental anti-Marcionite thesis: one God, Creator and Redeemer

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian argues that OT prophecy is fulfilled in Christ's incarnation, proving the Creator God sent him; Christ took real flesh from the virgin and truly suffered.
notes: OT fulfillment in Christ's incarnation; virgin birth; real flesh against Marcion

excerpt: Having taken the bread and given it to his disciples, he made it his own body by saying 'This is my body,' that is, the figure of my body. A figure, however, there could not have been unless there were first a veritable body.
notes: Eucharistic language in anti-Marcionite context; 'figure of my body' presupposes real body; debated realist vs figurative reading

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian defends the resurrection of the flesh by arguing from Paul's statements in 1 Corinthians 15 that the same body which dies will be raised and transformed.
notes: Bodily resurrection of the flesh defended against Marcionite and Gnostic denial

excerpt: We do indeed believe that there is only one God, but under this dispensation, or oikonomia as we call it, the one God has also a Son, his Word, who proceeded from himself, by whom all things were made. Him we believe to have been sent by the Father into the virgin and to have been born of her — God and man, Son of God and Son of man. We believe him to have suffered, died, and been buried, to have risen again, to sit at the right hand of the Father, and to come to judge. He also sent from heaven the Holy Spirit, the Paraclete, the sanctifier of the faith of those who believe in the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
notes: The rule of faith stated in trinitarian form; three persons, one God; oikonomia; foundational Latin trinitarian statement

excerpt: Thus the connection of the Father in the Son, and of the Son in the Paraclete, produces three coherent persons, who are yet distinct one from another. These three are one essence (una substantia), not one person (unus), as it is said: I and my Father are one — in respect of unity of substance, not singularity of number.
notes: Tres personae una substantia — the classic Latin trinitarian formula; one substance, three persons

excerpt: Bear always in mind that this is the rule of faith which I profess: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit are inseparable from each other. The Father is one, the Son another, the Spirit another — they are distinct, not separate. The Father is not the same as the Son, since they differ one from another in the mode of their being.
notes: Distinct persons but inseparable; anti-modalist; trinitarian distinctions

excerpt: We are a body knit together by a common religious profession, by unity of discipline, and by the bond of a common hope. We meet together as an assembly and a congregation. We pray together, we read scripture, we offer exhortation. Our presidents are elders, approved men. There is no buying and selling of any sort in the things of God. Even if there is a chest of sorts, it is not made up of purchase-money. On the monthly day, each puts in a small donation, if he will, and only if he be willing; for no one is compelled. These gifts are not spent on feasts or drinking bouts but to support and bury poor people, to supply the wants of boys and girls destitute of means and parents, and of old persons confined to the house.
notes: Description of Christian assembly, common fund, and community life in Carthage

excerpt: We multiply whenever we are mown down by you: the blood of Christians is seed. Your cruelty, though each act of it ever so extravagant, does not avail you; it is rather a temptation to the sect. We spring up in greater numbers the more we are cut down.
notes: Semen est sanguis Christianorum; martyrdom as witness and growth of the church

excerpt: Happy is our sacrament of water, in that by washing away the sins of our early blindness, we are set free and admitted into eternal life. How mighty is the grace of water, in the sight of God and his Christ, for the confirmation of baptism!
notes: Baptism as sacrament of water; washing away sins; admission to eternal life

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian states that the formula of baptism was commanded by the Lord: 'Go, teach the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.'
notes: Trinitarian baptismal formula commanded by Christ; Matt 28:19 as baptismal mandate

excerpt: And so, according to the circumstances and disposition, and even age, of each individual, the delay of baptism is preferable; principally, however, in the case of little children. For why is it necessary — if baptism itself be not so necessary — that the sponsors likewise should be thrust into danger? Let them come, then, while they are growing up; let them come while they are learning, while they are being taught whither to come; let them become Christians when they have become able to know Christ.
notes: Recommends delaying baptism of children; earliest explicit argument against routine infant baptism

excerpt: Not that in the waters we obtain the Holy Spirit; but in the water we are cleansed and prepared for the Holy Spirit. In like manner, after coming out from the laver we are thoroughly anointed with a blessed unction, and then the hand is laid on us through the benediction, inviting and welcoming the Holy Spirit.
notes: Baptismal anointing and imposition of hands; Spirit given post-baptism; baptismal ritual described

excerpt: The Son of God was crucified; I am not ashamed because men must needs be ashamed of it. And the Son of God died; it is by all means to be believed because it is absurd. And he was buried and rose again; the fact is certain because it is impossible. But how will all this be true in him, if he was not himself true — if he had not truly in himself that which might be crucified, might die, might be buried and might rise again? This is the flesh suffused with blood, built up with bones, interwoven with nerves, entwined with veins.
notes: Certum est quia impossibile; defense of the reality of Christ's flesh and incarnation; anti-docetist

excerpt: Shall it be held lawful to make an occupation of the sword, when the Lord proclaims that he who uses the sword shall perish by the sword? And shall the son of peace take part in the battle when it does not become him even to sue at law? Shall he apply the chain, and the prison, and the torture, and the punishment, who is not the avenger even of his own wrongs? Shall he stand guard for others more than for Christ? Do we believe it lawful for a human oath to be superadded to one divine?
notes: Christians must not serve in the military; incompatibility of military oath, sword, and punishment with Christian faith

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian opens with the statement that the Spirit of God, the Word of God, the Reason of God — the Word of Reason, the Reason and Spirit of the Word — Jesus Christ our Lord, determined a new form of prayer for the new disciples of the new covenant.
notes: Lord's Prayer as new form of prayer given by Christ; earliest systematic commentary on the Our Father

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian teaches that after baptism, God has provided one further repentance (exomologesis) for those who fall into grave sin. This second repentance is a painful public confession before the congregation, including fasting, weeping, and prostration; it is the last remaining refuge for the sinner, but it does exist.
notes: One post-baptismal repentance allowed through exomologesis (public confession); early penitential discipline

excerpt: The rule of faith is altogether one, alone immovable and irreformable: to believe in one God omnipotent, maker of the world; and in his Son Jesus Christ, born of the virgin Mary, crucified under Pontius Pilate, on the third day raised from the dead, received in the heavens, sitting now at the right hand of the Father, and coming to judge the living and the dead through the resurrection of the flesh.
notes: The rule of faith stated; creedal summary; apostolic tradition; irreformable confession

excerpt: The apostles first bore witness to the faith in Christ Jesus throughout Judaea and founded churches there; then went out into the world and preached the same doctrine of the same faith to the nations. They likewise founded churches in every city, from which the other churches borrowed the tradition of the faith and the seeds of doctrine, and are every day borrowing them to become churches. And therefore these churches are counted apostolic, being the offspring of apostolic churches. Every kind of thing must be classed according to its origin. These churches then are apostolic, in that they are derived from apostolic churches.
notes: Apostolic succession of churches; tradition transmitted through founding churches; seed metaphor

excerpt: Come now, you who wish to exercise your curiosity, run over the apostolic churches, in which the very seats of the apostles still preside at their own places; in which their authentic letters are read. Achaia is close to you — you have Corinth. If you are not far from Macedonia — you have Philippi, you have the Thessalonians. If you can go to Asia — you have Ephesus. But if you are near to Italy — you have Rome, whence our own authority derives also.
notes: Apostolic sees as living witnesses to the faith; Rome, Corinth, Philippi, Ephesus named; authority of apostolic churches

excerpt: The resurrection of the dead is the Christian's trust. By believing it we are what we claim to be. It is the truth which compels faith. And the truth God reveals. But the vulgar, and especially the heretics, disparage the resurrection.
notes: Bodily resurrection as central Christian identity; truth revealed by God; against heretical deniers

excerpt: Paraphrase: Tertullian contrasts the pagan spectacles with the eschatological spectacle that awaits believers — the coming of the Lord, the resurrection, the judgment — urging Christians to wait for these true spectacles rather than debasing themselves in the arena and theater.
notes: Eschatological spectacle vs earthly spectacles; moral argument for abstaining from games

excerpt: When entering the water, we professed the Christian faith in the words of its rule; we testified with our own mouth that we renounced the devil and his pomp and his angels. Well, is it not in connection with this engagement that we are forbidden the spectacles? The shows belong to the pomp and works of the devil.
notes: Baptismal renunciation includes spectacles; spectacles are the devil's pomp

excerpt: God made us of nothing; and just as not existing before we were made, so also being able to be made again after dissolution, as his will is able to make us. For what is impossible with man is possible with God.
notes: God who created from nothing can resurrect the body from dissolution; resurrection defended

excerpt: God, then, having his own Word internal within his own bowels, begat him, emitting him along with his own wisdom before all things. He had this Word as a helper in the things that were created by him, and by him he made all things. He is called governing principle because he rules and is lord of all things fashioned by him.
notes: Logos begotten from God before creation; Logos as helper and ruler of creation; pre-Nicene Logos theology

excerpt: And thus also the three days which were before the luminaries are types of the Trinity — of God, and his Word, and his wisdom.
notes: First Christian explicit use of Trinity (Τριάς) applied to Father, Word, and Wisdom; Theophilus's Trinity terminology

excerpt: To Titus, my true son in our common faith: Grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior.
Paraphrase: Paul says that he left Titus in Crete to set in order what remained and appoint elders in every town.
Paraphrase: Paul directs Titus to come to him at Nicopolis because he has decided to spend the winter there.

Paraphrase: Emperor Constantius II wrote to the rulers of Aksum Ezana and Sayzana requesting that Frumentius be sent to Egypt to have his faith examined by George of Alexandria the Arian bishop replacing Athanasius. The rulers did not comply.
notes: Letter of Constantius II to Aksumite rulers; attests Frumentius as bishop and Arian pressure on Aksum.

excerpt: Paraphrase: The text sets out the final war of the sons of light against the sons of darkness.
Paraphrase: The ungodly will come to shame in the day of reckoning and their hopes will perish.
